FELIX HOLT , 
 the RADICAL 

 BY 
 GEORGE ELIOT 

 upon the midland now the industrious muse doth fall , 
 the shire which we the heart of England well may call . 

 my native country thou , which so brave spirit hast breed , 
 if there be virtue yet remain in the earth , 
 or any good of thine thou bred'st into my birth , 
 accept it as thine own , whilst now I sing of thee , 
 of all thy later brood the unworthi though I be . 
  Drayton ; Polyolbion . 

 INTRODUCTION . 

 five - and - thirty year ago the glory have not yet depart from the old coach road : the great roadside inn be still brilliant with well - polish tankard , the smile glance of pretty barmaid , and the repartee of jocose hostler ; the mail still announce itself by the merry note of the horn ; the hedge - cutter or the rick - thatcher might still know the exact hour by the unfailing yet otherwise meteoric apparition of the pea - green Tally - ho or the yellow Independent ; and elderly gentleman in pony - chaise , quarter nervously to make way for the rolling , swing swiftness , have not cease to remark that time be finely change since they use to see the pack - horse and hear the tinkling of their bell on this very highway . 

 in those day there be pocket borough , a Birmingham unrepresented in Parliament and compel to make strong representation out of it , unrepeale corn - law , three - and - sixpenny letter , a brawny and many - breed pauperism , and other depart evil ; but there be some pleasant thing , too , which have also depart . Non omnia grandior ata qua fugiamus habet , say the wise goddess : you have not the good of it in all thing , o youngster ! the elderly man have his enviable memory , and not the least of they be the memory of a long journey in mid - spring or autumn on the outside of a stage coach . Posterity may be shoot , like a bullet through a tube , by atmospheric pressure , from Winchester to Newcastle : that be a fine result to have among our hope ; but the slow , old fashioned way of get from one end of our country to the other be the well thing to have in the memory . the tube - journey can never lend much to picture and narrative ; it be as barren as an exclamatory o ! whereas , the happy outside passenger , seat on the box from the dawn to the gloaming , gather enough story of english life , enough of english labor in town and country , enough aspect of earth and sky , to make episode for a modern odyssey . suppose only that his journey take he through that central plain , water at one extremity by the Avon , at the other by the Trent . as the morning silver the meadow with their long line of bushy willow mark the water - course , or burnish the 4   golden corn - rick cluster near the long roof of some midland homestead , he see the full - uddered cow drive from their pasture to the early milking . perhaps it be the shepherd , head - servant of the farm , who drive they , his sheep - dog follow with a heedless , unofficial air , as of a beadle in undress . the shepherd , with a slow and slouch walk , time by the walk of graze beast , move aside , as if unwillingly , throw out a monosyllabic hint to his cattle ; his glance , accustom to rest on thing very near the earth , seem to lift itself with difficulty to the coachman . mail or stage coach for he belong to the mysterious distant system of thing call " Gover'ment , " which , whatever it might be , be no business of his , any more than the most outlying nebula or the coal - sack of the southern hemisphere : his solar system be the parish ; the master 's temper and the casualty of lamb - time be his region of storm . he cut his bread and bacon with his pocket - knife , and feel no bitterness except in the matter of pauper laborer and the bad - luck that send contrarious season and the sheep - rot . he and his cow be soon leave behind , and the homestead , too , with its pond overhang by elder - tree , its untidy kitchen - garden and cone - shape yew - tree arbor . but everywhere the bushy hedgerow waste the land with their straggle beauty , shroud the grassy border of the pasture with catkine hazel , and toss their long blackberry branch on the corn - field . perhaps they be white with May , or star with pale pink dog - rose ; perhaps the urchin be already nut among they , or gather the plenteous crab . it be worth the journey only to see those hedgerow , the liberal home of unmarketable beauty of the purple blossom , ruby - berry nightshade , of the wild convolvulus climbing and spread in tendriled strength till it make a great curtain of pale - green heart and white trumpet , of the many - tube honey - suckle which , in its most delicate fragrance , hide a charm more subtle and penetrate than beauty . even if it be winter , the hedgerow show their coral , the scarlet haw , the deep - crimson hip , with linger brown leaf to make a resting - place for the jewel of the hoar - frost . such hedgerow be often as tall as the laborer ' cottage dot along the lane , or cluster into a small hamlet , their little dingy window telling , like thick - film eye , of nothing but the darkness within . the passenger on the coach - box , bowl 5   along above such a hamlet , see chiefly the roof of it : probably it turn its back on the road , and seem to lie away from everything but its own patch of earth and sky , away from the parish church by long field and green lane , away from all intercourse except that of tramp . if its face could be see , it be most likely dirty ; but the dirt be protestant dirt , and the big , bold , gin - breathing tramp be protestant tramp . there be no sign of superstition near , no crucifix or image to indicate a misguided reverence : the inhabitant be probably so free from superstition that they be in much less awe of the parson than of the overseer . yet they be save from the excess of Protestantism by not know how to read , and by the absence of handloom and mine to be the pioneer of Dissent : they be keep safely in the via medium of indifference , and could have register themselves in the census by a big black mark as member of the Church of England . 

 but there be trim cheerful village too , with a neat or handsome parsonage and gray church set in the midst ; there be the pleasant tinkle of the blacksmith 's anvil , the patient cart horse wait at his door ; the basket - maker peel his willow wand in the sunshine ; the wheelwright put his last touch to a blue cart with red wheel ; here and there a cottage with bright transparent window show pot full of bloom balsam or geranium , and little garden in front all double daisy or dark wallflower ; at the well , clean and comely woman carry yoked bucket , and toward the free school small Britons dawdle on , and handle their marble in the pocket of unpatched corduroy adorn with brass button . the land around be rich and marly , great corn - stack stand in the rick - yard for the rick - burner have not find their way hither ; the homestead be those of rich farmer who pay no rent , or have the rare advantage of a lease , and could afford to keep the corn till price have rise . the coach would be sure to overtake some of they on their way to their outlying field or to the market - town , sit heavily on their well - groom horse , or weigh down one side of an olive - green gig . they probably think of the coach with some contempt , as an accommodation for people who have not their own gig , or who , want to travel to London and such distant place , belong to the trading and less solid part of the nation . the passenger on the box could see that this be the district of protuberant optimist , sure that old England be the good of all possible country , and that if there be any fact which have not fall under their own 6   observation , they be fact not worth observe : the district of clean little market - town without manufacture , of fat living , an aristocratic clergy , and low poor - rate . but as the day wear on the scene would change : the land would begin to be blacken with coal - pit , the rattle of handloom to be hear in hamlet and village . here be powerful man walk queerly with knee bent outward from squat in the mine , go home to throw themselves down in their blacken flannel and sleep through the daylight , then rise and spend much of their high wage at the ale - house with their fellow of the Benefit Club ; here the pale eager face of the handloom - weaver , man and woman , haggard from sit up late at night to finish the week 's work , hardly begin till the Wednesday . everywhere the cottage and the small child be dirty , for the languid mother give their strength to the loom ; pious dissent woman , perhaps , who take life patiently , and think that salvation depend chiefly on predestination , and not at all on cleanliness . the gable of dissent chapel now make a visible sign of religion , and of a meeting - place to counterbalance the ale - house , even in the hamlet ; but if a couple of old termagant be see tear each other 's cap , it be a safe conclusion that , if they have not receive the sacrament of the Church , they have not at least give in to schismatic rite , and be free from the error of Voluntaryism . the breath of the manufacture town , which make a cloudy day and a red gloom by night on the horizon , diffuse itself over all the surround country , fill the air with eager unrest . here be a population not convince that old England be as good as possible ; here be multitudinous man and woman aware that their religion be not exactly the religion of their ruler , who might therefore be well than they be , and who , if well , might alter many thing which now make the world perhaps more painful than it need be , and certainly more sinful . yet there be the gray steeple too , and the churchyard , with their grassy mound and venerable headstone , sleep in the sunlight ; there be broad field and homestead , and fine old wood cover a rise ground , or stretch far by the roadside , allow only peep at the park and mansion which they shut in from the work day world . in these midland district the traveller pass rapidly from one phase of english life to another : after look down on a village dingy with coal - dust , noisy with the shaking of loom , he might skirt a parish all of field ,   7   high hedge , and deep rut lane ; after the coach have rattle over the pavement of a manufacturing town , the scene of riot and trade - union meeting , it would take he in another ten minute into a rural region , where the neighborhood of the town be only feel in the advantage of a near market for corn , cheese , and hay , and where man with a considerable banking account be accustom to say that " they never meddle with politic themselves . " the busy scene of the shuttle and the wheel , of the roar furnace , of the shaft and the pulley , seem to make but crowded nest in the midst of the large - space , slow - move life of homestead and far - away cottage and oak - shelter park . look at the dwelling scatter amongst the woody flat and the plow upland , under the low gray sky which overhang they with an unchanging stillness as if Time itself be pause , it be easy for the traveller to conceive that town and country have no pulse in common , except where the handloom make a far - reach straggling fringe about the great centre of manufacture ; that till the agitation about the Catholics in ' 29 , rural Englishmen have hardly know more of Catholics than of the fossil mammal ; and that their notion of Reform be a confused combination of rick - burner , trade - union , Nottingham riot , and in general whatever require the calling out of the yeomanry . it be still easy to see that , for the most part , they resist the rotation of crop and stand by their fallow : and the coachman would perhaps tell how in one parish an innovating farmer , who talk of Sir Humphrey Davy , have be fairly drive out by popular dislike , as if he have be a confounded Radical ; and how , the parson have one Sunday preach from the word , " break up your fallow - ground , " the people think he have make the text out of his own head , otherwise it would never have come ' so pat " on a matter of business ; but when they find it in the Bible at home , some say it be an argument for fallow   else why should the Bible mention fallow ? , but a few of the weak sort be shake , and think it be an argument that fallow should be do away with , else the Bible would have say , " let your fallow lie " ; and the next morning the parson have a stroke of apoplexy , which , as coincident with a dispute about fallow , so set the parish against the innovate farmer and the rotation of crop , that he could stand his ground no long , and transfer his lease . 

  8 

 the coachman be an excellent travel companion and commentator on the landscape : he could tell the name of site and person , and explain the meaning of group , as well as the shade of Virgil in a more memorable journey ; he have as many story about parish , and the man and woman in they , as the Wanderer in the " Excursion , " only his style be different . his view of life have originally be genial , such as become a man who be well warm within and without , and hold a position of easy , undisputed authority ; but the recent initiation of railway have embitter he : he now , as in a perpetual vision , see the ruin country strew with shattered limb , and regard Mr. Huskisson 's death as a proof of God 's anger against Stephenson . " why , every inn on the road would be shut up ! " and at that word the coachman look before he with the blank gaze of one who have drive his coach to the outermost edge of the universe , and see his leader plunge into the abyss . still he would soon relapse from the high prophetic strain to the familiar one of narrative . he know whose the land be wherever he drive ; what nobleman have half - ruin themselves by gambling ; who make handsome return of rent ; and who be at dagger - draw with his eld son . he perhaps remember the father of actual baronet , and know story of their extravagant or stingy housekeeping ; whom they have marry , whom they have horsewhip , whether they be particular about preserve their game , and whether they have have much to do with canal company . about any actual land proprietor he could also tell whether he be a Reformer or an anti - Reformer . that be a distinction which have " turn up " in latter time , and along with it the paradox , very puzzling to the coachman 's mind , that there be man of old family and large estate who vote for the Bill . he do not grapple with the paradox ; he let it pass , with all the discreetness of an experienced theologian or learn scholiast , prefer to point his whip at some object which could raise no question . 

 no such paradox trouble our coachman when , leave the town of Treby Magna behind he , he drive between the hedge for a mile or so , cross the queer long bridge over the river Lapp , and then put his horse to a swift gallop up the hill by the low - nestle village of Little Treby , till they be on the fine level road , skirt on one side by grand larch , oak , and wych elm , which sometimes open so far as to let the traveller see that there be a park behind they .

 how many time in the year , as the coach roll past the neglect - look lodge which interrupt the screen of tree , and show the river wind through a finely - timbere park , have the coachman answer the same question , or tell the same thing without be question ! that ? oh , that be Transome Court , a place there have be a fine sight of lawsuit about . generation back , the heir of the Transome name have somehow bargain away the estate , and it fall to the Durfeys , very distant connection , who only call themselves Transomes because they have get the estate . but the Durfeys ' claim have be dispute over and over again ; and the coachman , if he have be ask , would have say , though he might have to fall down dead the next minute , that property do not   always get into the right hand . however , the lawyer have find their luck in it ; and people who inherit estate that be lawe about often live in they as poorly as a mouse in a hollow cheese ; and , by what he could make out , that have be the way with these present durfey , or Transomes , as they call themselves . as for Mr. Transome , he be as poor , half - witte a fellow as you would wish to see ; but she be master , have come of a high family , and have a spirit you might see it in her eye and the way she sit her horse . forty year ago , when she come into this country , they say she be a pictur ' ; but her family be poor , and so she take up with a hatchet - face fellow like this Transome . and the eld son have be just such another as his father , only bad a wild sort of half - natural , who get into bad company . they say his mother hat he and wish he dead ; for she would get another son , quite of a different cut , who have go to foreign part when he be a youngster , and she want she favorite to be heir . but heir or no heir , Lawyer Jermyn have have his picking out of the estate . not a door in his big house but what be the fine polished oak , all get off the Transome estate . if anybody like to believe he pay for it , they be welcome . however , Lawyer Jermyn have sit on that box - seat many and many a time . he have make the will of most people thereabout . the coachman would not say that Lawyer Jermyn be not the man he would choose to make his own will some day . it be not so well for a lawyer to be over - honest , else he might not be up to other people 's trick . and as for the Transome business , there have be in and out in time go by , so that you could not   look into it straight backward . at this Mr. Sampson   everybody in North Loamshire know Sampson 's coach   would screw his 10   feature into a grimace expressive of entire neutrality , and appear to aim his whip at a particular spot on the horse 's flank . if the passenger be curious for further knowledge concern the Transome affair , Sampson would shake his head and say there have be fine story in his time ; but he never condescend to state what the story be . some attribute this reticence to a wise incredulity , other to a want of memory , other to simple ignorance . but at least Sampson be right in say that there have be fine story mean , ironically , story not altogether creditable to the party concern . 

 and such story often come to be fine in a sense that be not ironical . for there be seldom any wrong - doing which do not carry along with it some downfall of blindly - climb hope , some hard entail of suffering , some quickly - satiate desire that survive , with the life in death of old paralytic vice , to see itself curse by its woeful progeny some tragic mark of kinship in the one brief life to the far - stretch life that go before , and to the life that be to come after , such as have raise the pity and terror of man ever since they begin to discern between will and destiny . but these thing be often unknown to the world ; for there be much pain that be quite noiseless ; and vibration that make human agony be often a mere whisper in the roar of hurry existence . there be glance of hatred that stab and raise no cry of murder ; robbery that leave man or woman forever beggar of peace and joy , yet keep secret by the sufferer commit to no sound except that of low moan in the night , see in no writing except that make on the face by the slow month of suppress anguish and early morning tear . many an inherit sorrow that have mar a life have be breathe into no human ear . 

 the poet have tell we of a dolorous enchant forest in the under world . the thorn - bush there , and the thick - bark stem , have human history hide in they ; the power of unuttered cry dwell in the passionless - seem branch , and the red warm blood be darkly feed the quivering nerve of a sleepless memory that watch through all dream . these thing be a parable .

 CHAPTER I. 

 he leave I when the down upon his lip 
 Lay like the shadow of a hover kiss . 
 " beautiful mother , do not grieve , " he say ; 
 " I will be great , and build our fortune high . 
 and you shall wear the long train at court , 
 and look so queenly , all the lord shall say , 
 ' she be a royal changeling : there be some crown 
 lack the right head , since hers wear naught but braid . ' " 
 O , he be come now but I be gray : 
 and he 
 on the first of September , in the memorable year 1832 , some one be expect at Transome Court . as early as two o'clock in the afternoon the aged lodge - keeper have open the heavy gate , green as the tree trunk be green with nature 's powdery paint , deposited year after year . already in the village of Little Treby , which lie on the side of a steep hill not far off the lodge - gate , the eld matron sit in their good gown at the few cottage door border the road , that they might be ready to get up and make their courtesy when a travel carriage should come in sight ; and beyond the village several small boy be station on the look - out , intend to run a race to the barn - like old church , where the sexton wait in the belfry ready to set the one bell in joyful agitation just at the right moment . 

 the old lodge - keeper have open the gate and leave it in the charge of his lame wife , because he be want at the Court to sweep away the leaf , and perhaps to help in the stable . for though Transome Court be a large mansion , build in the fashion of Queen Anne 's time , with a park and ground as fine as any to be see in Loamshire , there be very few servant about it . especially , it seem , there must be a lack of gardener ; for , except on the terrace surround with a stone parapet in front of the house , where there be a parterre , keep with some neatness , grass have spread itself over the gravel walk , and over all the low mound once carefully cut as black bed for the shrub and large plant . many of the window have the shutter close , and under the grand Scotch fir that stoop toward one corner , the brown fir - needle of many year lie in a small stone balcony in front of two such darken window . all round , both near and far , there be grand tree , motionless in the still sunshine , and , like all large motionless thing , seem to add to the stillness . here and there a leaf flutter down ; petal fall in a silent shower ; a heavy moth flutter by , and , when it settle , seem to fall wearily ; the tiny bird alight on the walk , and hop about in perfect tranquillity ; even a stray rabbit sit 12   nibble a leaf that be to its liking , in the middle of a grassy space , with an air that seem quite impudent in so timid a creature . no sound be to be hear louder than a sleepy hum , and the soft monotony of run water hurrying on to the river that divide the park . stand on the south or east side of the house , you would never have guess that an arrival be expect . 

 but on the west side , where the carriage entrance be , the gate under the stone archway be throw open ; and so be the double door of the entrance - hall , let in the warm light on the scagliola pillar , the marble statue , and the broad stone staircase , with its mat wear into large hole . and , strong sign of expectation than all , from one of the door that surround the entrance - hall , there come forth from time to time a lady , who walk lightly over the polished stone floor , and stand on the door - step and watch and listen . she walk lightly , for her figure be slim and finely form , though she be between fifty and sixty . she be a tall , proud - look woman , with abundant gray hair , dark eye and eyebrow , and a somewhat eagle - like yet not unfeminine face . her tight - fit black dress be much worn ; the fine lace of her cuff and collar , and of the small veil that fall backward over her high comb , be visibly mend ; but rare jewel flash on her hand , which lie on her fold black - clothe arm like finely - cut onyx cameo . 

 meantime Mrs. Transome go to the door - step , watch and listen in vain . each time she return to the same room ; it be a moderate - sized comfortable room , with low ebony bookshelf round it , and it form an ante - room to a large library , of which a glimpse could be see through an open doorway , partly obstruct by a heavy tapestry curtain draw on one side . there be a great deal of tarnished gilding and dinginess on the wall and furniture of this small room , but the picture above the bookcase be all of a cheerful kind : portrait in pastel of pearly - skin lady with hair - powder , blue ribbon , and low bodice ; a splendid portrait in oil of a Transome in the gorgeous dress of the restoration ; another of a Transome in his boyhood , with his hand on the neck of a small pony ; and a large Flemish battle - piece , where war seem only a picturesque blue - and - red accident in a vast sunny expanse of plain and sky . probably such cheerful picture have be choose because this be Mrs. Transome 's usual sitting - room : it be certainly for this reason that , near the chair in which she seat herself each time she 13   re - enter , there hang a picture of a youthful face which bear a strong resemblance to her own : a beardless but masculine face , with rich brown hair hang low on the forehead , and undulate beside each cheek down to the loose white cravat . near this same chair be her write table , with vellum - cover account - book on it , the cabinet in which she keep she neatly - arrange drug , her basket for her embroidery , a folio volume of architectural engraving from which she take her embroidery - pattern , a number of the " North Loamshire Herald , " and the cushion for her fat Blenheim , which be too old and sleepy to notice its mistress 's restlessness . for , just now , Mrs. Transome could not abridge the sunny tedium of the day by the feeble interest of her usual indoor occupation . her consciousness be absorb by memory and prospect , and except that she walk to the entrance - door to look out , she sit motionless with fold arm , involuntarily from time to time turn toward the portrait close by she , and as often , when its young brown eye meet her , turn away again with self - check resolution . 

 at last , prompt by some sudden thought or by some sound , she rise and go hastily beyond the tapestry curtain into the library . she pause near the door without speak : apparently she only wish to see that no harm be be do . a man near seventy than sixty be in the act of range on a large library - table a series of shallow drawer , some of they contain dry insect , other mineralogical specimen . his pale mild eye , recede low jaw , and slight frame , could never have express much vigor , either bodily or mental ; but he have now the unevenness of gait and feebleness of gesture which tell of a past paralytic seizure . his threadbare clothe be thoroughly brush : his soft white hair be carefully part and arrange : he be not a neglect - look old man ; and at his side a fine black retriever , also old , sit on its haunch , and watch he as he go to and fro . but when Mrs. Transome appear within the doorway , her husband pause in his work and shrink like a timid animal look at in a cage where flight be impossible . he be conscious of a troublesome intention , for which he have be rebuke before that of disturb all his specimen with a view to a new arrangement . 

 after an interval , in which his wife stand perfectly still , observe he , he begin to put back the drawer in their place in the row of cabinet which extend under the bookshelf at one end of the 14   library . when they be all put back and close , Mrs. Transome turn away , and the frightened old man seat himself with Nimrod the retriever on an ottoman . peep at he again , a few minute after , she see that he have his arm round Nimrod 's neck , and be utter his thought to the dog in a loud whisper , as little child do to any object near they when they believe themselves unwatche . 

 at last the sound of the church - bell reach Mrs. Transome 's ear , and she know that before long the sound of wheel must be within hearing ; but she do not at once start up and walk to the entrance - door . she sit still , quiver and listening ; her lip become pale , her hand be cold and trembling . be her son really come ? she be far beyond fifty ; and since her early gladness in this well - love boy , the harvest of her life have be scanty . could it be that now when her hair be gray , when sight have become one of the day 's fatigue , when her young accomplishment seem almost ludicrous , like the tone of her first harpsichord and the word of the song long brown with age she be go to reap an assured joy ? to feel that the doubtful deed of her life be justify by the result , since a kind Providence have sanction they ? to be no long tacitly pity by her neighbor for her lack of money , her imbecile husband , her graceless eldest - bear , and the loneliness of her life ; but to have at her side a rich , clever , possibly a tender , son ? yes ; but there be the fifteen year of separation , and all that have happen in that long time to throw she into the background of her son 's memory and affection . and yet do not man sometimes become more filial in their feeling when experience have mellow they , and they have themselves become father ? still , if Mrs. Transome have expect only her son , she would have tremble less ; she expect a little grandson also : and there be reason why she have not be enrapture when her son have write to she only when he be on the eve of return that he already have an heir bear to he . 

 but the fact must be accept as they stand , and , after all , the chief thing be to have her son back again . such pride , such affection , such hope as she cherish in this fifty - sixth year of her life , must find their gratification in he or nowhere . once more she glance at the portrait . the young brown eye seem to dwell on her pleasantly ; but , turn from it with a sort of impatience , and say aloud , " of course 15   he will be alter ! " she rise almost with difficulty , and walk more slowly than before across the hall to the entrance - door . 

 already the sound of wheel be loud upon the gravel . the momentary surprise of see that it be only a post - chaise , without a servant or much luggage , that be pass under the stone archway and then wheel round against the flight of stone step , be at once merge in the sense that there be a dark face under a red travel - cap look at she from the window . she see nothing else ; she be not even conscious that the small group of her own servant have muster , or that old Hickes the butler have come forward to open the chaise door . she hear herself call " Mother ! " and feel a light kiss on each cheek ; but strong than all that sensation be the consciousness which no previous thought could prepare she for , that this son who have come back to she be a stranger . three minute before , she have fancy that , in spite of all change work by fifteen year of separation , she should clasp her son again as she have do at their parting ; but in the moment when their eye meet , the sense of strangeness come upon she like a terror . it be not hard to understand that she be agitated , and the son lead she across the hall to the sitting - room , close the door behind they . then he turn toward she and say , smile 

 " you would not have know I , eh , mother ? " 

 it be perhaps the truth . if she have see he in a crowd , she might have look at he without recognition not , however , without startled wonder ; for though the likeness to herself be no long strike , the year have overlay it with another likeness which would have arrest she . before she answer he , his eye , with a keen restlessness , as unlike as possible to the linger gaze of the portrait , have travel quickly over the room , alight on she as she say 

 " everything be change , Harold . I be an old woman , you see . " 

 " but straighter and more upright than some of the young one ! " say Harold ; inwardly , however , feel that age have make his mother 's face very anxious and eager . " the old woman at Smyrna be like sack . you have   not get clumsy and shapeless . how be it I have the trick of get fat ? "   here Harold lift his arm and spread out his plump hand .   " I remember my father be as thin as a herring . how be my father ? where be he ? " 

 Mrs. Transome just point to the curtain doorway , and let her son pass through it alone . she be not give to tear : but now , under the 16   pressure of emotion that could find no other vent , they burst forth . she take care that they should be silent tear , and before Harold come out of the library again they be dry . Mrs. Transome have not the feminine tendency to seek influence through pathos ; she have be use to rule in virtue of acknowledge superiority . the consciousness that she have to make her son 's acquaintance , and that her knowledge of the youth of nineteen might help she little in interpret the man of thirty - four , have fall like lead on her soul ; but in this new acquaintance of theirs she care especially that her son , who have see a strange world , should feel that he be come home to a mother who be to be consult on all thing , and who could supply his lack of the local experience necessary to an english landholder . her part in life have be that of the clever sinner , and she be equip with the view , the reason , and the habit which belong to that character ; life would have little meaning for she if she be to be gently thrust aside as a harmless elderly woman . and besides , there be secret which her son must never know . so , by the time Harold come from the library again , the trace of tear be not discernible , except to a very careful observer . and he do not observe his mother carefully ; his eye only glance at she on their way to the North Loamshire Herald , lie on the table near she , which he take up with his left hand , as he say 

 " Gad ! what a wreck poor father be ! paralysis , eh ? terribly shrink and shake crawl about among his book and beetle as usual , though . well , it be a slow and easy death . but he be not much over sixty - five , be he ? " 

 " Sixty - seven , count by birthday ; but your father be bear old , I think , " say Mrs. Transome , a little flushed with the determination not to show any unasked for feel . her son do not notice she . all the time he have be speak his eye have be run down the column of the newspaper . 

 " but your little boy , Harold where be he ? how be it he have not come with you ? " 

 " oh , I leave he behind , in town , " say Harold , still look at the paper . " my man Dominic will bring he , with the rest of the luggage . ah , I see it be young debarry , and not my old friend Sir Maximus , who be offer himself as candidate for North Loamshire . " 

 " yes . you do not answer I when I write to you to London about your standing . there be no other Tory candidate speak of , and you would have all the Debarry interest . " 17 

 " I hardly think that , " say Harold , significantly . 

 " why ? Jermyn say a Tory candidate can never be get in without it . " 

 " but I shall not be a Tory candidate . " 

 Mrs. Transome feel something like an electric shock . 

 " what then ? " she say , almost sharply . " you will not call yourself a Whig ? " 

 " God forbid ! I be a Radical . " 

 Mrs. Transome 's limb totter ; she sink into a chair . here be a distinct confirmation of the vague but strong feeling that her son be a stranger to she . here be a revelation to which it seem almost as impossible to adjust her hope and notion of a dignified life as if her son have say that he have be convert to Mahometanism at Smyrna , and have four wife , instead of one son , shortly to arrive under the care of Dominic . for the moment she have a sickening feeling that it be of no use that the long - delay good fortune have come at last all of no use though the unloved Durfey be dead and bury , and though Harold have come home with plenty of money . there be rich Radicals , she be aware , as there be rich Jews and dissenter , but she have never think of they as county people . Sir Francis Burdett have be generally regard as a madman . it be well to ask no question , but silently to prepare herself for anything else there might be to come . 

 " will you go to your room , Harold , and see if there be anything you would like to have alter ? " 

 " yes , let we go , " say Harold , throw down the newspaper , in which he have be rapidly read almost every advertisement while his mother have be go through her sharp inward struggle . " Uncle Lingon be on the bench still , I see , " he go on , as he follow she across the hall ; " be he at home will he be here this evening ? " 

 " he say you must go to the rectory when you want to see he . you must remember you have come back to a family with old - fashioned notion . your uncle think I ought to have you to myself in the first hour or two . he remember that I have not see my son for fifteen year . " 

 " ah , by Jove ! fifteen year so it be ! " say Harold , take his mother 's hand and draw it under his arm ; for he have perceive that her word be charge with an intention . " and you be as straight as an arrow 18   still ; you will carry the shawl I have bring you as well as ever . " 

 they walk up the broad stone step together in silence . under the shock of discover her son 's Radicalism , Mrs. Transome have no impulse to say one thing rather than another ; as in a man who have just be brand on the forehead all wonte motif would be uproot . Harold , on his side , have no wish oppose to filial kindness , but his busy thought be determine by habit which have no reference to any woman 's feeling ; and even if he could have conceive what his mother 's feeling be , his mind , after that momentary arrest , would have dart forward on its usual course . 

 " I have give you the south room , Harold , " say Mrs. Transome , as they pass along a corridor light from above and line with old family picture . " I think they would suit you well , as they all open into each other , and this middle one will make a pleasant sitting - room for you . "

 " what ! you have   be rather pinch , eh ? "

I always mean to be an Englishman , and thrash a lord or two who thrash I at Eton . "

 Mrs. Transome bite her lip , and turn away to draw up a blind . she would not reply to word which show how completely any conception of herself and her feeling be exclude from her son 's inward world .

 as she turn round again she say , " I suppose you have be use to great luxury ; these room look miserable to you , but you can soon make any alteration you like . "

I suppose if I ring there be some fellow who can act as valet and learn to attend to my hookah ? "

 " he will be here to breakfast at nine . but I leave you to Hickes now ; we dine in an hour . " 

 Mrs. Transome go away and shut herself in her own dressing - room . it have come to pass now this meeting with the son who have be the object of so much longing ; whom she have long for before he be bear , for whom she have sin , from whom she have wrench herself with pain at their parting , and whose come again have be the one great hope of her year . the moment be go by ; there have be no ecstasy , no gladness even ; hardly half an hour have pass , and few word have be speak , 21   yet with that quickness in weave new future which belong to woman whose action have keep they in habitual fear of consequence , Mrs. Transome think she see with all the clearness of demonstration that her son 's return have not be a good for she in the sense of make she any happy . 

 she stand before a tall mirror , go close to it and look at her face with hard scrutiny , as if it be unrelated to herself . no elderly face can be handsome , look at in that way ; every little detail be startlingly prominent , and the effect of the whole be lose . she see the dry - up complexion , and the deep line of bitter discontent about the mouth .

 she turn away from the mirror and walk up and down her room . 

 " what a likeness ! " she say , in a loud whisper ; " yet , perhaps , no one will see it besides I . "

where man and woman who have the soft bed and the most delicate eating , who have a very large share of that sky and earth which some be bear to have no more of than the fraction to be get in a crowded entry , yet grow haggard , fevere , and restless , like those who watch in other lottery . day after day , year after year , have yield blank ; new care have come , bring other desire for 22   result quite beyond her grasp , which must also be watch for in the lottery ; and all the while the round - limbe pet have be grow into a strong youth , who like many thing well than his mother 's caress , and who have a much keener consciousness of his independent existence than of his relation to she : the lizard 's egg , that white round passive prettiness , have become a brown , darting , determined lizard . the mother 's love be at first an absorbing delight , blunt all other sensibility ; Mrs. Transome have darkly feel the pressure of that unchangeable fact . yet she have clung to the belief that somehow the possession of this son be the good thing she live for ; to believe otherwise would have make her memory too ghastly a companion . some time or other , by some mean , the estate she be struggle to save from the grasp of the law would be Harold 's . somehow the hat Durfey , the imbecile eld , who seem to have become tenacious of a despicable squandering life , would be get rid of ; vice might kill he . meanwhile the estate be burden : there be no good prospect for any heir . Harold must go and make a career for himself and this be what he be bent on , with a precocious clearness of perception as to the condition on which he could hope for any advantage in life . like most energetic nature , he have a strong faith in his luck ; he have be gay at their parting , and have promise to make his fortune ; and in spite of past disappointment , Harold 's possible fortune still make some ground for his mother to plant her hope in . his luck have not fail he ; yet nothing have turn out accord to her expectation . her life have be like a spoiled shabby pleasure - day , in which the music and the procession be all miss , and nothing be leave at evening but the weariness of strive after what have be fail of . Harold have go with the Embassy to Constantinople , under the patronage of a high relative , his mother 's cousin ; he be to be diplomatist , and work his way upward in public life . but his luck have take another shape : he have save the life of an armenian banker , who in gratitude have offer he a prospect which his practical mind have prefer to the problematic promise of diplomacy and high - bear cousinship . Harold have become a merchant and banker at Smyrna ; and let the year pass without care to find the possibility of 23   visit his early home , and have show no eagerness to make his life at all familiar to his mother , ask for letter about England , but write scantily about himself . Mrs. Transome have keep up the habit of write to her son , but gradually the unfruitful year have dull her hope and yearning ; increase anxiety about money have worry she , and she be more sure of be fret by bad news about her dissolute eld son than of hear anything to cheer she from Harold . she have begin to live merely in small immediate care and occupation , and like all eager - minded woman who advance in life without any activity of tenderness or any large sympathy , she have contract small rigid habit of thinking and acting , she have she " way " which must not be cross , and have learn to fill up the great void of life with give small order to tenant , insist on medicine for infirm cottager , win small triumph in bargain and personal economy , and parry ill - natured remark of Lady Debarry 's by lancet - edge epigram . so her life have go on till more than a year ago , when that desire which have be so hungry when she be a bloom young mother , be at last fulfil at last , when her hair be gray , and her face look bitter , restless , and unenjoying , like her life . the news come from Jersey that Durfey , the imbecile son , be dead . now Harold be heir to the estate ; now the wealth he have gain could release the land from its burden ; now he would think it worth while to return home . a change have come over her life , and the sunlight break the cloud at evening be pleasant , though the sun must sink before long . hope , affection , the sweet part of her memory , start from their wintry sleep , and it once more seem a great good to have have a second son who in some way have cost she dearly . but again there be condition she have not reckon on . when the good tiding have be send to Harold , and he have announce that he would return so soon as he could wind up his affair , he have for the first time inform his mother that he have be marry , that his greek wife be no long live , but that he should bring home a little boy , the fine and most desirable of heir and grandson . Harold seat in his distant Smyrna home consider that he be take a rational view of what thing must have become by this time at the old place in England , when he figure his mother as a good elderly lady , who would necessarily be delighted with the possession on any term of a healthy grandchild , and would not mind much about the particular of a long - conceal marriage . 24 

 Mrs. Transome have tear up that letter in a rage . but in the month which have elapse before Harold could actually arrive , she have prepare herself as well as she could to suppress all reproach or query which her son might resent , and to acquiesce in his evident wish . the return be still look for with longing ; affection and satisfied pride would again warm her later year . she be ignorant what sort of man Harold have become now , and of course he must be change in many way ; but though she tell herself this , still the image that she know , the image fondness clung to , necessarily prevail over the negative insist on by her reason . 

 and so it be , that when she have move to the door to meet he , she have be sure that she should clasp her son again , and feel that he be the same who have be her boy , her little one , the love child of her passionate youth . an hour seem to have change everything for she . a woman 's hope be weave of sunbeam ; a shadow annihilate they . the shadow which have fall over Mrs. Transome in this first interview with her son be the presentiment of her powerlessness . if thing go wrong , if Harold get unpleasantly dispose in a certain direction where her chief dread have always lain , she seem to foresee that her word would be of no avail . the keenness of her anxiety in this matter have serve as insight ; and Harold 's rapidity , decision , and indifference to any impression in other , which do not far or impede his own purpose , have make themselves feel by she as much as she would have feel the unmanageable strength of a great bird which have alight near she , and allow she to stroke its wing for a moment because food lie near she . 

 under the cold weight of these thought Mrs. Transome shiver . that physical reaction rouse she from her reverie , and she could now hear the gentle knock at the door to which she have be deaf before . notwithstanding her activity and the fewness of her servant , she have never dress herself without aid ; nor would that small , neat , exquisitely clean old woman who now present herself have wish that her labor should be save at the expense of such a sacrifice on her lady 's part . the small old woman be Mrs. Hickes , the butler 's wife , who act as housekeeper , lady's - maid , and superintendent of the kitchen the large stony scene of inconsiderable cooking . forty year ago she have enter Mrs. Transome 's service , when that lady 25   be beautiful Miss Lingon , and her mistress still call she Denner , as she have do in the old day . 

 " the bell have ring , then , Denner , without my hear it ? " say Mrs. Transome , rise . 

 " yes , madam , " say Denner , reach from a wardrobe an old black velvet dress trim with much - mend point , in which Mrs. Transome be will not to look queenly of an evening . 

 Denner have still strong eye of that short - sighted kind which see through the narrow chink between the eyelash . the physical contrast between the tall , eagle - face , dark - eyed lady , and the little peer wait woman , who have be round - feature and of pale mealy complexion from her youth up , have doubtless have a strong influence in determine Denner 's feeling toward her mistress , which be of that worshipful sort pay to a goddess in age when it be not think necessary or likely that a goddess should be very moral . there be different order of being so run Denner 's creed and she belong to another order than that to which her mistress belong . she have a mind as sharp as a needle , and would have see through and through the ridiculous pretension of a bear servant who do not submissively accept the rigid fate which have give she bear superior . she would have call such pretension the wriggling of a worm that try to walk on its tail . there be a tacit understanding that Denner know all her mistress 's secret , and her speech be plain and unflattering ; yet with wonderful subtlety of instinct she never say anything which Mrs. Transome could feel humiliate by , as by familiarity from a servant who know too much .  peer into Mrs. Transome 's face she see clearly that the meeting with the son have be a disappointment in some way . she speak with a refined accent , in a low quick , monotonous tone 

 " Mr. Harold be dress ; he shake I by the hand in the corridor , and be very pleasant . " 

 " what an alteration , Denner ! no likeness to I now . "

 Mrs. Transome know perfectly that Denner have divine her thought . 

 " I do not   know how thing will go on now , but it seem something too good to happen that they will go on well . I be afraid of ever expect anything good again . "

 " oh , there be pleasure in know one be not a fool , like half the people one see about . and manage one 's husband be some pleasure ; and do all one 's business well . why , if I have   only get some orange flower to candy , I should not   like to die till I see they all right . then there be the sunshine now and then ; I like that as the cat do .
