 the conversation have in this way break up into tete - a - tete , and the hilarity of the evening might be consider a failure . still the punch be drunk , the account be duly swell , and , notwithstanding the innovate spirit of the time , Sir Maximus Debarry 's establishment be keep up in sound hereditary british manner . 

 CHAPTER VIII . 

 " Rumor doth double like the voice and echo . " 
  Shakespeare . 
 the mind of a man be as a country which be once open to squatter , who have breed and multiply and become master of the land . but then happeneth a time when new and hungry comer dispute the land ; and there be trial of strength , and the strong win . nevertheless the first squatter be they who have prepare the ground , and the crop to the end will be sequent   though chiefly on the nature of the soil , as of light sand , mixed loam , or heavy clay , yet   somewhat on the primal labor and sowing . 

 that talkative maiden , Rumor , though in the interest of art she be figure as a youthful , wing beauty with flow garment , soar above the head of man , and breathe world - thrill news through a gracefully - curve trumpet , be in fact a very old maid , who pucker her silly face by the fireside , and really do no more than chirp a wrong guess or a lame story into the ear of a fellow gossip ; all the rest of the work attribute to she be do by the ordinary working of those passion against which man pray in the Litany , with the help of a plentiful stupidity against which we have never yet have any authorized form of prayer . 

 when Mr. Scales 's strong need to make an impressive figure in conversation , together with his very slight need of any other premise than his own sense of his wide general knowledge and probable infallibility , lead he to specify five hundred thousand as the low admissible amount of Harold Transome 's commercially - acquire fortune , it be not fair to put this down to poor old Miss Rumor , who have only tell Scales that the fortune be considerable . and again , when the curt Mr. Sircome find occasion at Treby to mention the five hundred thousand 97   as a fact that folk seem pretty sure about , this expansion of the butler into " folk " be entirely due to Mr. Sircome 's habitual preference for word which could not be lay hold of or give people a handle over he . it be in this simple way that the report of Harold Transome 's fortune spread and be magnify , add much lustre to his opinion in the eye of Liberals , and compelling even man of the opposite party to admit that it increase his eligibility as a member for North Loamshire . it be observe by a sound thinker in these part that property be ballast ; and when once the aptness of that metaphor have be perceive , it follow that a man be not fit to navigate the sea of politic without a great deal of such ballast ; and that , rightly understand , whatever increase the expense of election , inasmuch as it virtually raise the property qualification , be an unspeakable boon to the country . 

 meanwhile the fortune that be get large in the imagination of constituent be shrink a little in the imagination of its owner . it be hardly more than a hundred and fifty thousand ; and there be not only the heavy mortgage to be pay off , but also a large amount of capital be need in order to repair the farm - building all over the estate , to carry out extensive drainage , and make allowance to in - come tenant , which might remove the difficulty of newly let the farm in a time of agricultural depression . the farm actually tenant be hold by man who have beg hard to succeed their father in get a little poor every year , on land which be also get poor , where the high rate of increase be in the arrear of rent , and where the master , in crushed hat and corduroy , look pitiably lean and care - wear by the side of pauper laborer , who show that superior assimilate power often observe to attend nourishment by the public money . Mr. Goffe , of Rabbit 's end , have never have it explain to he that , accord to the true theory of rent , land must inevitably be give up when it would not yield a profit equal to the ordinary rate of interest ; so that from want of know what be inevitable , and not from a titanic spirit of opposition , he keep on his land . he often say to himself , with a melancholy wipe of his sleeve across his brow , that he " do not   know which - a - way to turn " ; and he would have be still more at a loss on the subject if he have quit Rabbit 's end with a wagonful of furniture and utensil , a file of receipt , a wife with five child , and a shepherd dog in low spirit . 

  98 

 it take no long time for Harold Transome to discover this state of thing , and to see , moreover , that , except on the demesne immediately around the house , the timber have be mismanage . the wood have be recklessly thin , and there have be insufficient planting . he have not yet thoroughly investigate the various account keep by his mother , by Jermyn , and by bank the bailiff ; but what have be do with the large sum which have be receive for timber be a suspicious mystery to he . he observe that the farm hold by Jermyn be in first - rate order , that a good deal have be spend on the building , and that the rent have stand unpaid . Mrs. Transome have take an opportunity of say that Jermyn have have some of the mortgage deed transfer to he , and that his rent be set against so much interest . Harold have only say , in his careless yet decisive way , " oh , Jermyn be hang ! it seem to I if Durfey have not   die and make room for I , Jermyn would have end by come to live here , and you would have have to keep the lodge and open the gate for his carriage . but I shall pay he off mortgage and all by - and - by . I will owe he nothing not even a curse ! " Mrs. Transome say no more . Harold do not care to enter fully into the subject with his mother . the fact that she have be active in the management of the estate have ride about it continually , have busy herself with account , have be head - bailiff of the vacant farm , and have yet allow thing to go wrong be set down by he simply to the general futility of woman 's attempt to transact man 's business . he do not want to say anything to annoy she : he be only determined to let she understand , as quietly as possible , that she have well cease all interference . 

 Mrs. Transome do understand this ; and it be very little that she dare to say on business , though there be a fierce struggle of her anger and pride with a dread which be nevertheless supreme . as to the old tenant , she only observe , on hear Harold burst forth about their wretched condition , " that with the estate so burden , the yearly loss by arrear could well be bear than the outlay and sacrifice necessary in order to let the farm anew . " 

 " I be really capable of calculate , Harold , " she end , with a touch of bitterness . " it seem easy to deal with farmer and their affair when you only see they in print , I dare say ; but it be not quite so easy when you live among they . you have only to look at Sir Maximus 's estate : you will see plenty of the same thing . the time have be dreadful 99   and old family like to keep their old tenant . but I dare say that be Toryism . " 

 " it be a hash of odd and end , if that be Toryism , my dear mother . however , I wish you have keep three more old tenant ; for then I should have have three more fifty - pound voter . and , in a hard run , one may be beat by a head . but , " Harold add , smile and hand she a ball of worst which have fall , " a woman ought to be a tory , and graceful , and handsome , like you . I should hate a woman who take up my opinion and talk for I . I be an oriental , you know . I say , mother , shall we have this room furnish with rose - color ? I notice that it suit your bright gray hair . " 

 Harold think it be only natural that his mother should have be in a sort of subjection to Jermyn throughout the awkward circumstance of the family . it be the way of woman , and all weak mind , to think that what they have be use to be unalterable , and any quarrel with a man who manage private affair be necessarily a formidable thing . he himself be proceed very cautiously , and prefer not even to know too much just at present , lest a certain personal antipathy he be conscious of toward Jermyn , and an occasional liability to exasperation , should get the well of a calm and clear - sighted resolve not to quarrel with the man while he could be of use . Harold would have be disgust with himself if he have help to frustrate his own purpose . and his strong purpose now be to get return for Parliament , to make a figure there as a liberal member , and to become on all ground a personage of weight in North Loamshire . 

 how Howard Transome come to be a Liberal in opposition to all the tradition of his family , be a more subtle enquiry than he have ever care to follow out . the newspaper undertake to explain it . the North Loamshire Herald witness with a grief and disgust certain to be share by all person who be actuate by wholesome british feeling , an example of defection in the inheritor of a family name which in time past have be associate with attachment to right principle , and with the maintenance of our constitution in Church and State ; and point to it as an additional proof that man who have pass any large portion of their life beyond the limit of our favored country , usually contract not only a laxity of feel toward Protestantism , nay , toward religion itself a latitudinarian spirit hardly distinguishable from atheism but also a levity of disposition , induce they to tamper with those institution by which alone Great Britain have rise to her pre - eminence among the nation . such man , infect with outlandish 100   habit , intoxicate with vanity , grasp at momentary power by flattery of the multitude , fearless because godless , Liberal because un - English , be ready to pull one stone from under another in the national edifice , till the great structure totter to its fall . on the other hand , the Duffield Watchman see in this signal instance of self - liberation from the trammel of prejudice , a decisive guarantee of intellectual pre - eminence , unite with a generous sensibility to the claim of man as man , which have burst asunder , and cast off , by a spontaneous exertion of energy , the cramping out - wear shell of hereditary bias and class interest . 

 but these large - minded guide of public opinion argue from wide datum than could be furnish by any knowledge of the particular case concern . Harold Transome be neither the dissolute cosmopolitan so vigorously sketch by the Tory Herald , nor the intellectual giant and moral lobster suggest by the liberal imagination of the Watchman . twenty year ago he have be a bright , active , good - temper lad , with sharp eye and a good aim ; he delight in success and in predominance ; but he do not long for an impossible predominance , and become sour and sulky because it be impossible . he play at the game he be clever in , and usually win ; all other game he let alone , and think they of little worth . at home and at Eton he have be side by side with his stupid eld brother Durfey , whom he despise ; and he very early begin to reflect that since this Caliban in miniature be old than himself , he must carve out his own fortune . that be a nuisance ; and on the whole the world seem rather ill - arrange , at Eton especially , where there be many reason why Harold make no great figure . he be not sorry the money be want to send he to Oxford ; he do not see the good of Oxford ; he have be surround by many thing during his short life , of which he have distinctly say to himself that he do not see the good , and he be not dispose to venerate on the strength of any good that other see . he turn his back on home very cheerfully , though he be rather fond of his mother , and very fond of Transome Court , and the river where he have be use to fish ; but he say to himself as he pass the lodge - gate , " I will get rich somehow , and have an estate of my own , and do what I like with it . " this determine aim at something not easy but clearly possible , mark the direction in which Harold 's nature be strong ; he have the energetic will and muscle , the 101   self - confidence , the quick perception , and the narrow imagination which make what be admiringly call the practical mind . 

 since then his character have be ripen by a various experience , and also by much knowledge which he have set himself deliberately to gain . but the man be no more than the boy writ large , with an extensive commentary . the year have nourish an inclination to as much opposition as would enable he to assert his own independence and power without throw himself into that tabooed condition which rob power of its triumph . and this inclination have help his shrewdness in form judgment which be at once innovate and moderate . he be addict at once to rebellion and to conformity , and only an intimate personal knowledge could enable any one to predict where his conformity would begin . the limit be not define by theory , but be draw in an irregular zigzag by early disposition and association ; and his resolution , of which he have never lose hold , to be a thorough Englishman again some day , have keep up the habit of consider all his conclusion with reference to english politic and english social condition . he mean to stand up for every change that the economical condition of the country require , and he have an angry contempt for man with coronet on their coach , but too small a share of brain to see when they have well make a virtue of necessity . his respect be rather for man who have no coronet , but who achieve a just influence by further all measure which the common - sense of the country , and the increase self - assertion of the majority , peremptorily demand . he could be such a man himself . 

 in fact Harold Transome be a clever , frank , good - natured egoist ; not stringently consistent , but without any disposition to falsity ; but with a pride that be mould in an individual rather than an hereditary form ; unspeculative , unsentimental , unsympathetic ; fond of sensual pleasure , but disinclined to all vice , and attach as a healthy , clear - sighted person , to all conventional morality , construe with a certain freedom , like doctrinal article to which the public order may require subscription . a character be apt to look but indifferently , write out in this way . reduce to a map , our premise seem insignificant , but they make , nevertheless , a very pretty freehold to live in and walk over ; and so , if Harold Transome have be among your acquaintance , and you have observe his quality through the medium of his agreeable person , bright smile , and a certain easy charm which 102   accompany sensuousness when unsullie by coarseness through the medium also of the many opportunity in which he would have make himself useful or pleasant to you you would have think he a good fellow , highly acceptable as a guest , a colleague , or a brother - in - law . whether all mother would have like he as a son be another question . 

 it be a fact perhaps keep a little too much in the background , that mother have a self large than their maternity , and that when their son have become tall than themselves , and be go from they to college or into the world , there be wide space of their time which be not fill with pray for their boy , read old letter , and envy yet bless those who be attend to their shirt - button . Mrs. Transome be certainly not one of those bland , adoring , and gently tearful woman . after share the common dream that when a beautiful man - child be bear to she , her cup of happiness would be full , she have travel through long year apart from that child to find herself at last in the presence of a son of whom she be afraid , who be utterly unmanageable by she , and to whose sentiment in any give case she possess no key . yet Harold be a kind son : he kiss his mother 's brow , offer she his arm , let she choose what she like for the house and garden , ask she whether she would have bay or gray for her new carriage , and be bent on see she make as good a figure in the neighborhood as any other woman of her rank . she tremble under this kindness : it be not enough to satisfy she ; still , if it should ever cease and give place to something else she be too uncertain about Harold 's feeling to imagine clearly what that something would be . the fine thread , such as no eye see , if bind cunningly about the sensitive flesh , so that the movement to break they would bring torture , may make a bad bondage than any fetter . Mrs. Transome feel the fatal thread about she , and the bitterness of this helpless bondage mingle itself with the new elegancie of the dining and drawing - room , and all the household change which Harold have order to be bring about with magical quickness . nothing be as she have once expect it would be . if Harold have show the least care to have her stay in the room with he if he have really care for her opinion if he have be what she have dream he would be in the eye of those people who have make her world if all the past could be dissolve , and leave 103   no solid trace of itself mighty if that be all impossible she would have taste some joy ; but now she begin to look back with regret to the day when she sit in loneliness among the old drapery , and still long for something that might happen . yet , save in a bitter little speech , or in a deep sigh , hear by no one besides Denner , she keep all these thing hide in her heart , and go out in the autumn sunshine to overlook the alteration in the pleasure - ground very much as a happy woman might have do . one day , however , when she be occupy in this way , an occasion come on which she choose to express indirectly a part of her inward care . 

 she be stand on the broad gravel in the afternoon ; the long shadow lie on the grass ; the light seem the more glorious because of the reddened and golden tree . the gardener be busy at their pleasant work ; the newly - turn soil give out an agreeable fragrance ; and little Harry be play with Nimrod round old Mr. Transome , who sit placidly on a low garden - chair . the scene would have make a charming picture of english domestic life , and the handsome , majestic , gray - haired woman   obviously grandmamma   would have be especially admire . but the artist would have feel it requisite to turn her face toward her husband and little grandson , and to have give she an elderly amiability of expression which would have divide remark with his exquisite rendering of her indian shawl . Mrs. Transome 's face be turn the other way , and for this reason she only hear an approach step , and do not see whose it be ; yet it startle she : it be not quick enough to be her son 's step , and besides , Harold be away at Duffield . it be Mr. Jermyn 's .

 Jermyn have turn round his savage side , and the blandness be out of sight . it be this that have always frighten Mrs. Transome : there be a possibility of fierce insolence in this man who be to pass with those near to she as her indebted servant , but whose brand she secretly bore . she be as powerless with he as she be with her own son .

"

 and in this way they part on the gravel , in that pretty scene where they have meet . Mrs. Transome shiver as she stand alone : all around she , where there have once be brightness and warmth , there be white ash , and the sunshine look dreary as it fall on they . 

 Mr. Jermyn 's heavy reflection in rid homeward turn on the possibility of incident between himself and Harold Transome which would have disagreeable result , require he to raise money , and perhaps cause scandal , which in its way might also help to create a monetary deficit . a man of sixty , with a wife whose Duffield connection be of the high respectability , with a family of tall daughter , an expensive establishment , and a large professional business , owe a great deal more to himself as the mainstay of all those solidity , than to feeling and idea which be quite unsubstantial . there be many unfortunate coincidence which place Mr. Jermyn in an uncomfortable position just now ; he have not be much to blame , he consider ; if it have not be for a sudden turn of affair no one would have complain . he defy any man to say that he have intend to wrong people ; he be able to refund , to make reprisal , if they could be fairly demand . only he would certainly have prefer that they should not be demand . 

 a german poet be entrust with a particularly fine sausage , which he be to convey to the donor 's friend at Paris . in the course of a long journey he smell the sausage ; he get hungry , and desire to taste it ; he par a morsel off , then another , and another , in successive moment of temptation , till at last the sausage be , humanly speak , at an end . the offence have not be premeditate . the poet have never love meanness , but he love sausage ; and the result be undeniably awkward .

 CHAPTER X. 

 " he make love neither with rose , nor with apple , nor with lock of hair . " Theocritus . 

 one Sunday afternoon Felix Holt rap at the door of Mr. Lyon 's house , although he could hear the voice of the minister preach in the chapel . he stand with a book under his arm , apparently confident that there be someone in the house to open the door for he . in fact , Esther never go to chapel in the afternoon : that " exercise " make her head ache .

 " how be I to oblige you ? by join the Church ? " 

 " no ; but by ask yourself whether life be not as solemn a thing as your father take it to be in which you may be either a blessing or a curse to many . you know you have never do that . you do not   care to be well than a bird trim its feather , and peck about after what please it . you be discontent with the world because you can not   get just the small thing that suit your pleasure , not because it be a world where myriad of man and woman be grind by wrong and misery , and taint with pollution . " 

 Esther feel her heart swell with mingled indignation at this liberty , wound pride at this depreciation , and acute consciousness that she could not contradict what Felix say . he be outrageously ill - bred ; but she feel that she should be lower herself by tell he so , and manifest her anger ; in that way she would be confirm his accusation of a littleness that shrink from severe truth ; and , besides , through all her mortification there pierce a sense that this exasperation of Felix against she be more complimentary than anything in his previous behavior . she have self - command enough to speak with her usual silvery voice . 

 " pray go on , Mr. Holt . relieve yourself of these burn truth . I be sure they must be troublesome to carry unuttered . " 

 " yes , they be , " say Felix , pausing , and stand not far off she . " I can not   bear to see you go the way of the foolish woman who spoil man 's life . man can not   help love they , and so they make themselves slave to the petty desire of petty creature . that be the way those who might do well spend their life for nought get check in every great effort toil with brain and limb for thing that have no more to do with a manly life than tart and confectionery . that be what make woman a curse ; and life be stunt to suit their littleness . that be why I will never love , if I can help it ; and if I love , I will bear it , and never marry . " 

 the tumult of feeling in Esther 's mind mortification , anger , the sense of a terrible power over she that Felix seem to have as his angry word vibrate through she be get almost too much for her self - control . she feel her lip quiver ; but her pride , which fear nothing so much as the betrayal of her emotion , help she to a desperate effort . she pinch her own hand hard to overcome her tremor , and say , in a tone of scorn 

 " I ought to be very much oblige to you for give I your confidence so freely . " 

  114 

 " ah ! now you be offend with I , and disgust with I . I expect it would be so . a woman do not   like a man who tell she the truth . " 

 " I think you boast a little too much of your truth - telling , Mr. Holt , " say Esther , flash out at last . " that virtue be apt to be easy to people when they only wound other and not themselves . tell the truth often mean no more than take a liberty . " 

 " yes , I suppose I should have be take a liberty if I have try to drag you back by the skirt when I see you run into a pit . " 

 " you should really find a sect . preach be your vocation . it be a pity you should ever have an audience of only one . " 

 " I see I have make a fool of myself . I think you have a more generous mind that you might be kindle to a well ambition . but I have   set your vanity aflame nothing else . I be go . good - bye . " 

 " good - bye , " say Esther , not look at he . he do not open the door immediately . he seem to be adjust his cap and pull it down . Esther long to be able to throw a lasso round he and compel he to stay , that she might say what she choose to he ; her very anger make this departure irritate , especially as he have the last word , and that a very bitter one . but soon the latch be lift and the door close behind he . she run up to her bedroom and burst into tear . poor maiden ! there be a strange contradiction of impulse in her mind in those first moment . she could not bear that Felix should not respect she , yet she could not bear that he should see she bend before his denunciation . she revolt against his assumption of superiority , yet she feel herself in a new kind of subjection to he . he be ill - bred , he be rude , he have take an unwarrantable liberty ; yet his indignant word be a tribute to she : he think she be worth more pain than the woman of whom he take no notice . it be excessively impertinent in he to tell she of his resolving not to love not to marry as if she care about that ; as if he think himself likely to inspire an affection that would incline any woman to marry he after such eccentric step as he have take . have he ever for a moment imagine that she have think of he in the light of a man who would make love to she ? but do he love she one little bit , and be that the reason why he want she to change ? Esther feel less angry at that form of freedom ; though she be quite sure that she do 115   not love he , and that she could never love any one who be so much of a pedagogue and master , to say nothing of his oddity . but he want she to change . for the first time in her life Esther feel herself seriously shake in her self - contentment . she know there be a mind to which she appear trivial , narrow , selfish . every word Felix have say to she seem to have burn itself into her memory . she feel as if she should forevermore be haunt by self - criticism , and never do anything to satisfy those fancy on which she have simply pique herself before without be dog by inward question . her father 's desire for her conversion have never move she ; she see that he adore she all the while , and he never check her unregenerate act as if they degrade she on earth , but only mourn over they as unfit she for heaven . Unfitness for heaven   speak of as " Jerusalem " and " glory " , the prayer of a good little father , whose thought and motif seem to she like the " life of Dr. Doddridge , " which she be content to leave unread , do not attack her self - respect and self - satisfaction . but now she have be sting sting even into a new consciousness concern her father . be it true that his life be so much worthy than her own ? she could not change for anything Felix say , but she tell herself he be mistaken if he suppose she incapable of generous thought . 

 she hear her father come into the house . she dry her tear , try to recover herself hurriedly , and go down to he . 

 " you want your tea , father ; how your forehead burn ! " she say gently , kiss his brow , and then put she cool hand on it . 

 Mr. Lyon feel a little surprise ; such spontaneous tenderness be not quite common with she ; it remind he of her mother . 

 " my sweet child , " he say gratefully , think with wonder of the treasure still leave in our fallen nature . 

 CHAPTER XI . 

 truth be the precious harvest of the earth . 
 but once , when harvest wave upon a land , 
 the noisome cankerworm and caterpillar , 
 locust , and all the swarm foul - bear brood , 
 fasten upon it with swift , greedy jaw , 
 and turn the harvest into pestilence , 
 until man say , what profit it to sow ? 
 Felix be go to Sproxton that Sunday afternoon . he always enjoy his walk to that outlying hamlet ; it take he   by a short cut   through 116   a corner of Sir Maximus Debarry 's park ; then across a piece of common , break here and there into red ridge below dark mass of furze ; and for the rest of the way alongside of the canal , where the Sunday peacefulness that seem to rest on the border meadow and pasture be hardly break if a horse pull into sight along the towing - path , and a boat , with a little curl of blue smoke issue from its tin chimney , come slowly glide behind . Felix retain something of his boyish impression that the day in a canal - boat be all like Sundays ; but the horse , if it have be put to he , would probably have prefer a more Judaic or Scotch rigor with regard to canal - boat , or at least that the Sunday towing should be do by ass , as a low order . 

 this canal be only a branch of the grand trunk , and end among the coal - pit , where Felix , cross a network of black tram - road , soon come to his destination that public institute of Sproxton , know to its frequenter chiefly as Chubb 's , but less familiarly as the Sugar Loaf , or the New pit ; this last be the name for the more modern and lively nucleus of the Sproxton hamlet . the other nucleus , know as the Old Pits , also support its " public , " but it have something of the forlorn air of an abandon capital ; and the company at the blue cow be of an inferior kind equal , of course , in the fundamental attribute of humanity , such as desire for beer , but not equal in ability to pay for it . 

 when Felix arrive , the great Chubb be stand at the door . Mr. Chubb be a remarkable publican ; none of your stock Bonifaces , red , bloated , jolly , and joke . he be thin and sallow , and be never , as his constant guest observe , see to be the bad   or the well   for liquor ; indeed , as among soldier an eminent general be hold to have a charmed life , Chubb be hold by the member of the Benefit Club to have a charmed sobriety , a vigilance over his own interest that resist all narcotic . his very dream , as state by himself , have a method in they beyond the wake thought of other man . Pharaoh 's dream , he observe , be nothing to they ; and , as lie so much out of ordinary experience , they be hold particularly suitable for narration on Sunday evening , when the listening collier , well wash and in their good coat , shake their head with a sense of peculiar edification which belong to the inexplicable . Mr. Chubb 's reason for become landlord of the Sugar Loaf , be found on the severe calculation . have an active mind , and be averse to bodily labor , he have thoroughly consider what 117   calling would yield he the good livelihood with the least possible exertion , and in that sort of line he have see that a " public " amongst miner who earn high wage be a fine opening . he have prosper accord to the merit of such judicious calculation , be already a forty - shilling freeholder , and be conscious of a vote for the county . he be not one of those mean - spirited man who find the franchise embarrassing , and would rather have be without it : he regard his vote as part of his investment , and mean to make the good of it . he call himself a straight - forward man , and at suitable moment express his view freely ; in fact , he be know to have one fundamental division for all opinion " my idee " and " humbug . " 

 when Felix approach , Mr. Chubb be stand , as usual , with his hand nervously busy in his pocket , his eye glance around with a detective expression at the black landscape , and his lipless mouth compress , yet in constant movement . on a superficial view it might be suppose that so eager - seem a personality be unsuited to the publican 's business ; but in fact , it be a great provocative to drink . like the shrill bite talk of a vixenish wife , it would have compel you to " take a little something " by way of dull your sensibility . 

 Hitherto , notwithstanding Felix drink so little ale , the publican treat he with high civility . the come election be a great opportunity for apply his political " idee , " which be , that society exist for the sake of the individual , and that the name of that individual be Chubb . now , for a conjunction of absurd circumstance inconsistent with that idea , it happen that Sproxton hereto have be somewhat neglect in the canvass . the head member of the company that work the mine be Mr. Peter Garstin , and the same company receive the rent from the Sugar Loaf . hence , as the person who have the most power of annoy Mr. Chubb , and be of detriment to he , Mr. Garstin be naturally the candidate for whom he have reserve his vote . but where there be this intention of ultimately gratify a gentleman by vote for he in an open british manner on the day of the poll , a man , whether Publican or Pharisee   Mr. Chubb use this generic classification of mankind as one that be sanction by scripture , be all the free in his relation with those delude person who take he for what he be not , and imagine he to be a waverer . but for some time opportunity have seem barren . there be but three dubious vote besides Mr. Chubb 's 118   in the small district of which the Sugar Loaf could be regard as the centre of intelligence and inspiration : the collier , of course , have no vote , and do not need political conversion ; consequently , the interest of Sproxton have only be tacitly cherish in the breast of candidate . but ever since it have be know that a radical candidate be in the field , that in consequence of this Mr. Debarry have coalesce with Mr. Garstin , and that Sir James Clement , the poor baronet , have retire , Mr. Chubb have be occupy with the most ingenious mental combination in order to ascertain what possibility of profit to the Sugar Loaf might lie in this altered state of the canvass . 

 he have a cousin in another county , also a publican , but in a large way , and resident in a borough , and from he Mr. Chubb have gather more detailed political information than he could find in the Loamshire newspaper . he be now enlighten enough to know that there be a way of use voteless miner and navvy at nomination and election . he approve of that ; it enter into his political " idee " ; and indeed he would have be for extend the franchise to this class at least in Sproxton . if any one have observe that you must draw a line somewhere , Mr. Chubb would have concur at once , and would have give permission to draw it at a radius of two mile from his own tap . 

 from the first Sunday evening when Felix have appear at the Sugar Loaf , Mr. Chubb have make up his mind that this ' cute man who keep himself sober be an electioneering agent . that he be hire for some purpose or other there be not a doubt ; a man do not   come and drink nothing without a good reason . in proportion as Felix 's purpose be not obvious to Chubb 's mind , it must be deep ; and this grow conviction have even lead the publican on the last Sunday evening privately to urge his mysterious visitor to let a little ale be chalk up for he it be of no consequence . Felix know his man , and have take care not to betray too soon that his real object be so to win the ear of the good fellow about he as to induce they to meet he on a Saturday evening in the room where Mr. Lyon , or one of his deacon , habitually hold his Wednesday preaching . only woman and child , three old man , a journeyman tailor , and a consumptive youth , attend those preaching ; not a collier have be win from the strong ale of the Sugar Loaf , not even a navvy from the muddy drink of the blue cow . Felix be sanguine ; he see some pleasant face among the miner when they be wash 119   on Sundays ; they might be teach to spend their wage well . at all event , he be go to try : he have great confidence in his power of appeal , and it be quite true that he never speak without arrest attention . there be nothing well than a dame school in the hamlet ; he think that if he could move the father , whose blacken week - day person and flannel cap , ornament with tallow candle by way of plume , be a badge of hard labor , for which he have a more sympathetic fibre than for any ribbon in the buttonhole if he could move these man to save something from their drink and pay a school - master for their boy , a great service would be do they than if Mr. Garstin and his company be persuade to establish a school . 

 " I will lay hold of they by their fatherhood , " say Felix ; " I will take one of their little fellow and set he in the midst . till they can show there be something they love well than swill themselves with ale , extension of the suffrage can never mean anything for they but extension of boozing . one must begin somewhere : I will begin at what be under my nose . I will begin at Sproxton . that be what a man would do if he have a red - hot superstition . can not   one work for sober truth as hard as for megrim ? " 

 Felix Holt have his illusion , like other young man , though they be not of a fashionable sort ; refer neither to the impression his costume and horsemanship might make on beholder , nor to the ease with which he would pay the Jews when he give a loose to his talent and apply himself to work . he have fix his choice on a certain Mike Brindle   not that Brindle be his real name each colly have his sobriquet   as the man whom he would induce to walk part of the way home with he this very evening , and get to invite some of his comrade for the next Saturday . Brindle be one of the head miner : he have a bright good - natured face , and have give especial attention to certain performance with a magnet which Felix carry in his pocket . 

 Mr. Chubb , who have also his illusion , smile graciously as the enigmatic customer come up to the door - step . 

 " well , sir , Sunday seem to be your day : I begin to look for you on a Sunday now . " 

 " yes , I be a workingman ; Sunday be my holiday , " say Felix , pause at the door since the host seem to expect this . 

 " ah , sir , there be many way of work . I look at it you be one of those as work with your brain . that be what I do myself . " 

  120 

 " one may do a good deal of that and work with one 's hand too . " 

 " ah , sir , " say Mr. Chubb , with a certain bitterness in his smile , " I have   that sort of head that I have   often wish I be stupider . I use thing up , sir ; I see into thing a deal too quick . I eat my dinner , as you may say , at breakfast - time . that be why I hardly ever smoke a pipe . no soon do I stick a pipe in my mouth than I puff and puff till it be go before other folk ' be well light ; and then , where be I ? I might as well have let it alone . in this world it be well not to be too quick . but you know what it be , sir . " 

 " not I , " say Felix , rub the back of his head , with a grimace . " I generally feel myself rather a blockhead . the world 's a largish place , and I have not   turn everything inside out yet . " 

 " ah , that be your deepness . I think we understand one another . and about this here election , I lie two to one we should agree if we be to come to talk about it . " 

 " ah ! " say Felix , with an air of caution . 

 " you be none of a Tory , eh , sir ? you will not   go to vote for debarry ? that be what I say at the very first go - off . say I , he be no Tory . I think I be right , sir eh ? " 

 " certainly ; I be no tory . " 

 " no , no , you do not   catch I wrong in a hurry . well , between you and I , I care no more for the Debarrys than I care for Johnny Groats . I live on none o ' their land , and not a pot's - worth do they ever send to the Sugar Loaf . I be not frightened at the Debarrys : there be no man more independent than I . I will plump or I will split for they as treat I the handsome and be the most of what I call gentleman ; that be my idee . and in the way of hatch for any man , they be fool that do not   employ I . " 

 we mortal sometimes cut a pitiable figure in our attempt at display . we may be sure of our own merit , yet fatally ignorant of the point of view from which we be regard by our neighbor . our fine pattern in tattooing may be far from throw he into a swoon of admiration , though we turn ourselves all round to show they . thus it be with Mr. Chubb . 

 " yes , " say Felix , dryly ; " I should think there be some sort of work for which you be just fit . " 

 " ah , you see that ? well , we understand one another . you be no tory ; no more be I. and if I would get four hand to show at a nomination , the Debarrys should not   have one of them . my idee be , there be a deal too much of their scutchin and their moniment in Treby Church . what be their 121   scutchin mean ? they be a sign with little liquor behind them ; that be how I take it . there be nobody can give account of them as I ever hear . " 

 Mr. Chubb be hinder from far explain his view as to the historical element in society by the arrival of new guest , who approach in two group . the foremost group consist of well - know collier , in their good Sunday beaver and colored handkerchief serve as cravat , with the long end float . the second group be a more unusual one , and cause Mr. Chubb to compress his mouth and agitate the muscle about it in rather an excited manner . 

 first come a smartly - dress personage on horseback , with a conspicuous expansive shirt - front and figured satin stock . he be a stout man , and give a strong sense of broadcloth . a wild idea shoot through Mr. Chubb 's brain ; could this grand visitor be Harold Transome ? excuse he : he have be give to understand by his cousin from the distant borough that a radical candidate in the condescension of canvassing have even go the length of eat bread - and - treacle with the child of an honest freeman , and declare his preference for that simple fare . Mr. Chubb 's notion of a Radical be that he be a new and agreeable kind of lick - spittle who fawn on the poor instead of on the rich , and so be likely to send customer to a " public " ; so that he argue well enough from the premise at his command . 

 the mount man of broadcloth have follower : several shabby - look man , and Sproxton boy of all size , whose curiosity have be stimulate by unexpected largesse . a stranger on horseback scatter half - pence on a Sunday be so unprecedented that there be no knowing what he might do next ; and the small hindmost fellow in sealskin cap be not without hope that an entirely new order of thing have set in . 

 everyone wait outside for the stranger to dismount , and Mr. Chubb advance to take the bridle . 

 " well , Mr. Chubb , " be the first word when the great man be safely out of the saddle , " I have   often hear of your fine tap , and I be come to taste it . " 

 " walk in , sir pray walk in , " say Mr. Chubb , give the horse to the stable - boy . " I shall be proud to draw for you . if anybody be be praise I , I think my ale will back he . " 

 all enter in the rear of the stranger except the boy , who peep in at the window . 

  122 

 " will not   you please to walk into the parlor , sir , " say Mr. Chubb , obsequiously . 

 " no , no , I will sit down here . this be what I like to see , " say the strange , look round at the collier , who eye he rather shyly " a bright hearth where workingmen can enjoy themselves . however , I will step into the other room for three minute , just to speak half a dozen word with you . " 

 Mr. Chubb throw open the parlor door , and then step back , take the opportunity of say , in a low tone , to Felix , " do you know this gentleman ? " 

 " not I ; no . " 

 Mr. Chubb 's opinion of Felix Holt sink from that moment . the parlor door be close , but no one sit down or order beer . 

 " I say , master , " say Mike Brindle , go up to Felix , " do not   you think that be one o ' the ' lection man ? " 

 " very likely . " 

 " I hear a chap say they be up and down everywhere , " say Brindle ; " and now be the time , they say , when a man can get beer for nothing . " 

 " ay , that be sin ' the reform , " say a big , red - whisker man , call Dredge . " that be bring the ' lection and the drink into these part ; for afore that , it be all kep ' up the Lord know wheer . " 

 " well , but the Reform 's niver come anigh Sprox'on , " say a gray - haired but stalwart man call Old Sleck . " I do not   believe nothing about'n , I do not . "

" well , I do . there 's folk will not   believe beyond the end o ' their own pickax . you can not   drive nothing into them , not if you split their skull . I know for certain sure , from a chap in the cartin ' way , as he be get money and drink too , only for holler . eh , master , what do you say ? " Brindle end , turn with some deference to Felix . 

 " should you like to know all about the reform ? " say Felix , use his opportunity . " if you would , I can tell you . " 

 " ay , ay tell 's ; you know I will be bind , " say several voice at once . 

 " ah , but it will take some little time . and we must be quiet . the cleverest of you those who be look up to in the Club must come and meet I at Peggy Button 's cottage next Saturday , at seven o'clock , after dark . and , Brindle , you must bring that little yellow - haired lad of your . and anybody that be get a little boy a very little fellow , who will not   understand what be say may bring   123   he . but you must keep it close , you know . we do not   want fool there . but everybody who hear I may come . I shall be at Peggy Button 's . " 

 " why , that be where the Wednesday preachin ' be , " say Dredge . " I have   be aforced to give my wife a black eye to hinder she from go to the preachin ' . Lors - a - massy , she think she know well nor I , and I can not   make head nor tail of her talk . " 

 " why can not   you let the woman alone ? " say Brindle , with some disgust . " I would be ashamed to beat a poor crawling thing 'cause she like preach . " 

 " no more I do beat she afore , not if she scrat ' I , " say Dredge , in vindication ; " but if she jabber at I , I can not   abide it . Howsomever , I will bring my Jack to Peggy 's o ' Saturday . his mother shall wash he . he be but four year old , and he will swear and square at I a good un , if I set he on . " 

 " there you go blatherin ' , " say Brindle , intend a mild rebuke . 

 this dialogue , which be in danger of become too personal , be interrupt by the reopening of the parlor door , and the reappearance of the impressive stranger with Mr. Chubb , whose countenance seem unusually radiant . 

 " sit you down here , Mr. Johnson , " say Chubb , move an arm - chair . " this gentleman be kind enough to treat the company , " he add , look round , " and what be more , he will take a cup with them ; and I think there be no man but what will say that be a honor . " 

 the company have nothing equivalent to a " hear , hear , " at command , but they perhaps feel the more , as they seat themselves with an expectation unvente by utterance . there be a general satisfactory sense that the hitherto shadowy Reform have at length come to Sproxton in a good round shape , with broadcloth and pocket . Felix do not intend to accept the treating , but he choose to stay and hear , take his pint as usual . 

 " Capital ale , capital ale , " say Mr. Johnson , as he set down his glass , speak in a quick , smooth treble . " now , " he go on , with a certain pathos in his voice , look at Mr. Chubb , who sit opposite , " there be some satisfaction to I in find an establishment like this at the pit . for what would high wage do for the workingman if he could not   get a good article for his money ? why , gentleman " here he look round " I have   be into ale - house where I have   see a fine fellow 124   of a miner or a stone - cutter come in and have to lay down money for beer that I should be sorry to give to my pig ! " here Mr. Johnson lean forward with square elbow , hand place on his knee , and a defiant shake of the head . 

 " aw , like at the blue cow , " fall in the irrepressible Dredge , in a deep bass ; but he be rebuke by a severe nudge from Brindle . 

 " yes , yes , you know what it be , my friend , " say Mr. Johnson , look at Dredge , and restore his self - satisfaction . " but it will not   last much long , that be one good thing . bad liquor will be sweep away with other bad article . trade will prosper and what be trade now without steam ? and what be steam without coal ? and mark you this , gentleman there be no man and no government can make coal . " 

 a loud " haw , haw , " show that this fact be appreciate . 

 " nor freeston ' , nayther , " say a wide - mouthed wiry man call Gills , who wish for an exhaustive treatment of the subject , be a stone - cutter . 

 " nor freestone , as you say ; else , I think , if coal could be make above - ground , honest fellow who be the pith of our population would not have to bend their back and sweat in a pit six day out of the seven . no , no ; I say , as this country prosper it have more and more need of you , sir . it can do without a pack of lazy lord and lady , but it can never do without brave collier . and the country will prosper . I pledge you my word , sir , this country will rise to the tiptop of everything , and there be not   a man in it but what shall have his joint in the pot , and his spare money jingle in his pocket , if we only exert ourselves to send the right man to Parliament man who will speak up for the collier , and the stone - cutter , and the navvy "   Mr. Johnson wave his hand liberally , " and will stand no nonsense . this be a crisis , and we must exert ourselves . we have   get reform , gentleman , but now the thing be to make reform work . it be a crisis I pledge you my word it be a crisis . " 

 Mr. Johnson throw himself back as if from the concussion of that great noun . he do not suppose that one of his audience know what a crisis mean ; but he have large experience in the effect of uncomprehende word ; and in this case the collier be throw into a state of conviction concern they do not know what , which be a fine preparation for " hit out , " or any other act carry a due sequence to such a conviction . 

  125 

 Felix feel himself in danger of get into a rage . there be hardly any mental misery bad than that of have our own serious phrase , our own rooted belief , caricature by a charlatan or a hireling . he begin to feel the sharp low edge of his tin pint - measure , and to think it a tempting missile . 

 Mr. Johnson certainly have some qualification as an orator . after this impressive pause he lean forward again , and say , in a lowered tone , look round 

 " I think you all know the good news . " 

 there be a movement of shoe - sol on the quarried floor , and a scrape of some chair leg , but no other answer . 

 " the good news I mean be , that a first - rate man , Mr. Transome , of Transome Court , have offer himself to represent you in Parliament , sir . I say you in particular , for what he have at heart be the welfare of the workingman of the brave fellow that wield the pickaxe , and the saw , and the hammer . he be rich have more money than Garstin but he do not   want to keep it to himself . what he want be , to make a good use of it , gentleman . he be come back from foreign part with his pocket full of gold . he could buy up the Debarrys , if they be worth buy , but he be get something well to do with his money . he mean to use it for the good of the workingman in these part . I know there be some man who put up for Parliament and talk a little too big . they may say they want to befriend the collier , for example . but I should like to put a question to they . I should like to ask they , ' what collier ? ' there be collier up at Newcastle , and there be collier down in Wales . will it do any good to honest Tom , who be hungry in Sproxton , to hear that Jack at Newcastle have his belly full of beef and pudding ? " 

 " it ought to do he good , " Felix burst in , with his loud , abrupt voice , in odd contrast with glib Mr. Johnson 's . " if he know it be a bad thing to be hungry and not have enough to eat , he ought to be glad that another fellow , who be not idle , be not suffer in the same way . " 

 every one be startle . the audience be much impressed with the grandeur , the knowledge , and the power of Mr. Johnson . his brilliant promise confirm the impression that Reform have at length reach the New pit ; and reform , if it be good for anything , must at last resolve itself into spare money mean ' sport " and drink , and keep away from work for several day in the week . these " brave " man of Sproxton like Felix as one of themselves , only much more knowing as a 126   workingman who have see many distant part , but who must be very poor , since he never drink more than a pint or so . they be quite inclined to hear what he have get to say on another occasion , but they be rather irritated by his interruption at the present moment . Mr. Johnson be annoy , but he speak with the same glib quietness as before , though with an expression of contempt . 

 " I call it a poor - spirited thing to take up a man 's straight - forward word and twist they . what I mean to say be plain enough that no man can be save from starve by look on while other eat . I think that be common - sense , eh , sir ? " 

 there be again an approve " Haw , haw . " to hear anything say , and understand it , be a stimulus that have the effect of wit . Mr. Chubb cast a suspicious and viperous glance at Felix , who feel that he have be a simpleton for his pain . 

 " well , then , " continue Mr. Johnson , " I suppose I may go on . but if there be any one here well able to inform the company than I be , I give way I give way . " 

 " Sir , " say Mr. Chubb , magisterially , " no man shall take the word out of your mouth in this house . and , " he add , look pointedly at Felix , " company that be get no more order to give , and want to turn up rusty to they that have , have well be make room than fill it . love an ' ' armony 's the word on our Club 's flag , an ' love an ' ' armony be the meaning of ' the Sugar Loaf , William Chubb . ' folk of a different mind have well seek another house of call . " 

 " very good , " say Felix , lay down his money and take his cap . " I be go . " he see clearly enough that if he say more , there would be a disturbance which could have no desirable end . 

 when the door have close behind he , Mr. Johnson say , " what be that person 's name ? " 

 " do anybody know it ? " say Mr. Chubb . 

 a few no be hear . 

 " I have   hear he speak like a downright Reformer , else I should have look a little sharp after he . but you may see he be nothing partic'lar . " 

 " it look rather bad that no one know his name , " say Mr. Johnson . " he be most likely a Tory in disguise a Tory spy . you must be careful , sir , of man who come to you and say they be Radicals , and yet do nothing for you . they will stuff you with word no lack of word but word be wind . now , a man like Transome come forward and say to the workingman of this country : ' here I be , ready to serve you and speak 127   for you in Parliament , and to get the law make all right for you ; and in the meanwhile , if there be any of you who be my neighbor who want a day 's holiday , or a cup to drink with friend , or a copy of the King 's likeness why , I be your man . I be not a paper handbill all word and no substance nor a man with land and nothing else ; I have   get bag of gold as well as land . ' I think you know what I mean by the King 's likeness . " 

 here Mr. Johnson take a half - crown out of his pocket and hold the head toward the company . 

 " well , sir , there be some man who like to keep this pretty picture a great deal too much to themselves . I do not   know whether I be right , but I think I have   hear of such a one not a hundred mile from here . I think his name be Spratt , and he manage some company 's coal - pit . " 

 " haw , haw ! Spratt Spratt 's his name , " be roll forth to an accompaniment of scrap shoe - sol . 

 " a screwing fellow , by what I understand a domineering fellow who would expect man to do as he like without pay they for it . I think there be not an honest man would not   like to disappoint such an upstart . " 

 there be a murmur which be interpret by Mr. Chubb . " I will answer for them , sir . " 

 " now , listen to I . here be Garstin : he be one of the company you work under . what be Garstin to you ? who see he ? and when they do see he they see a thin miserly fellow who keep his pocket button . he call himself a Whig , yet he will split vote with a Tory he will drive with the Debarrys . now , gentleman , if I say I would get a vote , and anybody ask I what I should do with it , I should say , ' I will plump for Transome . ' you have   get no vote , and that be a shame . but you will have some day , if such man as Transome be return ; and then you will be on a level with the first gentleman in the land , and if he want to sit in Parliament , he must take off his hat and ask your leave . but though you have not   get a vote you can give a cheer for the right man , and Transome be not a man like Garstin ; if you lose a day 's wage by give a cheer for Transome , he will make you amend . that be the way a man who have no vote can serve himself and his country ; he can lift up his hand and shout ' Transome forever ! ' ' hurray for Transome ! ' let the workingman let the collier and navvy and stone - cutter , who between you and I have a good deal too much the bad of it , as thing   128   be now let they join together and give their hand and voice for the right man , and they will make the great people shake in their shoe a little ; and when you shout for Transome , remember you shout for more wage , and more of your right , and you shout to get rid of rat and sprat and such small animal , who be the tool the rich make use of to squeeze the blood out of the poor man . " 

 " I wish there would be a row I would pommel he , " say Dredge , who be generally feel to be speak to the question . 

 " no , no , my friend there you be a little wrong . no pommel no striking first . there you have the law and the constable against you . a little rolling in the dust and knock hat off , a little pelt with soft thing that will stick and not bruise all that do not   spoil the fun . if a man be to speak when you do not   like to hear he , it be but fair you should give he something he do not   like in return . and the same if he be get a vote and do not   use it for the good of the country ; I see no harm in split his coat in a quiet way . a man must be teach what be right if he do not   know it . but no kick , no knock down , no pommelling .  " it ' ud be good fun , though , if so - be , " say Old Sleck , allow himself an imaginative pleasure .

I do not   say it will be the well for overlooker and screw , and rat and sprat ; but it will be the well for every good fellow who take his pot at the Sugar Loaf . " 

 Mr. Johnson 's exertion for the political education of the Sproxton man do not stop here , which be the more disinterested in he as he do not expect to see they again , and could only set on foot an organization by which their instruction could be continue without he . in this he be quite successful . a man know among the " butty " as Pack , who have already be mention by Mr. Chubb , presently join the party , and have a private audience of Mr. Johnson , that he might be institute as the ' shepherd " of this new flock .

 CHAPTER XII . 

 " oh , sir , ' twa that mixture of spite and over - feed merriment which pass for humor with the vulgar . in their fun , they have much resemblance to a turkey - cock . it have a cruel beak , and a silly iteration of ugly sound ; it spread its tail in self - glorification , but show you the wrong side of the ornament liking admiration , but know not what be admirable .  this Sunday evening , which promise to be so memorable in the experience of the Sproxton miner , have its drama also for those unsatisfactory object to Mr. Johnson 's moral sense , the Debarrys . certain incident occur at Treby Manor cause an excitement there which spread from the dining - room to the stable ; but no one undergo such agitate transition of feeling as Mr. Scales . at six o'clock that superior 130   butler be chuckle in triumph at have play a fine and original practical joke on his rival , Mr. Christian . some two hour after that time he be frightened , sorry , and even meek ; he be on the brink of a humiliating confession ; his cheek be almost livid ; his hair be flatten for want of due attention from his finger ; and the fine roll of his whisker , which be too firm to give way , seem only a sad reminiscence of past splendor and felicity . his sorrow come about in this wise . 

 after service on that Sunday morning , Mr. Philip Debarry have leave the rest of the family to go home in the carriage , and have remain at the rectory to lunch with his uncle Augustus , that he might consult he touch some letter of importance . he have return the letter to his pocket - book but have not return the book to his pocket , and he finally walk away leave the enclosure of private paper and bank - note on his uncle 's escritoire . after his arrival at home he be remind of his omission , and immediately dispatch Christian with a note beg his uncle to seal up the pocket - book and send it by the bearer . this commission , which be give between three and four o'clock , happen to be very unwelcome to the courier . the fact be that Mr. Christian , who have be remarkable through life for that power of adapt himself to circumstance which enable a man to fall safely on all - four in the most hurried expulsion and escape , be not exempt from bodily suffer a circumstance to which there be no known way of adapt one 's self so as to be perfectly comfortable under it , or to push it off on to other people 's shoulder . he do what he could : he take dos of opium when he have an access of nervous pain , and he console himself as to future possibility by think that if the pain ever become intolerably frequent , a considerable increase in the dose might put an end to they altogether . he be neither Cato nor Hamlet , and though he have learn their soliloquy at his first boarding - school , he would probably have increase his dose without recite those master - piece . next to the pain itself he dislike that any one should know of it : defective health diminish a man 's market value ; he do not like to be the object of the sort of pity he himself give to a poor devil who be force to make a wry face or " give in " altogether . 

 he have feel it expedient to take a slight dose this afternoon , and still he be not altogether relieve at the time he set off for the rectory . on return with the valuable case safely deposit in his hind 131   pocket , he feel increase bodily uneasiness , and take another dose . think it likely that he look rather pitiable , he choose not to proceed to the house by the carriage - road . the servant often walk in the park on a Sunday , and he wish to avoid any meeting . he would make a circuit , get into the house privately , and after deliver his packet to Mr. Debarry , shut himself up till the ringing of the half - hour bell . but when he reach an elbowed seat under some sycamore , he feel so ill at ease that he yield to the temptation of throw himself on it to rest a little . he look at his watch : it be but five ; he have do his errand quickly hitherto , and Mr. Debarry have not urge haste . but in less than ten minute he be in a sound sleep . certain condition of his system have determine a strong effect than usual from the opium . 

 as he have expect , there be servant stroll in the park , but they do not all choose the most frequented part . Mr. Scales , in pursuit of a light flirtation with the young lady 's maid , have prefer a more sequester walk in the company of that agreeable nymph . and it happen to be this pair , of all other , who alight on the sleep Christian a sight which at the very first moment cause Mr. Scales a vague pleasure as at an incident that must lead to something clever on his part . to play a trick , and make some one or other look foolish , be hold the most pointed form of wit throughout the back region of the Manor , and serve as a constant substitute for theatrical entertainment : what the farce want in costume or " make up " it gain in the reality of the mortification which excite the general laughter . and lo ! here be the offensive , the exasperatingly cool and superior Christian , catch comparatively helpless , with his head hang on his shoulder , and one coat - tail hang out heavily below the elbow of the rustic seat . it be this coat - tail which serve as a suggestion to Mr. Scales 's genius . put his finger up in warning to Mrs. Cherry , and say , " hush be quiet I see a fine bit of fun " he take a knife from his pocket , step behind the unconscious Christian , and quickly cut off the pendent coat - tail . scale know nothing of the errand to the rectory ; and as he notice that there be something in the pocket , think it be probably a large cigar - case . so much the well he have no time to pause . he throw the coat - tail as far as he could , and notice that it fall among the elm under which they have be walk . then , beckon to Mrs Cherry , he hurry away with 132   she toward the more open part of the park , not dare to explode in laughter until it be safe from the chance of wake the sleeper . and then the vision of the graceful , well - appoint Mr. Christian , who sneer at Scales about his " get up , " have to walk back to the house with only one tail to his coat , be a source of so much enjoyment to the butler , that the fair Cherry begin to be quite jealous of the joke . still she admit that it really be funny , titter intermittently , and pledge herself to secrecy . Mr. Scales explain to she that Christian would try to creep in unobserved , but that this must be make impossible ; and he request she to imagine the figure this interlope fellow would cut when everybody be ask what have happen . " Hallo , Christian ! where be your coat tail ? " would become a proverb at the Manor , where joke keep remarkably well without the aid of salt ; and Mr. Christian 's comb would be cut so effectually that it would take a long time to grow again . exit Scales , laughing , and present a fine example of dramatic irony to any one in the secret of Fate . 

 when Christian awake , he be shocked to find himself in the twilight . he start up , shake himself , miss something , and soon become aware what it be he miss . he do not doubt that he have be rob , and he at once foresaw that the consequence would be highly unpleasant . in no way could the cause of the accident be so represent to Mr. Philip Debarry as to prevent he from view his hitherto unimpeachable factotum in a new and unfavorable light . and though Mr. Christian do not regard his present position as brilliant , he do not see his way to anything well . a man nearly fifty who be not always quite well be seldom ardently hopeful : he be aware that this be a world in which merit be often overlook . with the idea of robbery in full possession of his mind , to peer about and search in the dimness , even if it have occur to he , would have seem a preposterous waste of time and energy . he know it be likely that Mr. Debarry 's pocket - book have important and valuable content , and that he should deepen his offence by defer his announcement of the unfortunate fact . he hasten back to the house , relieve by the obscurity from that mortification of his vanity on which the butler have count . indeed , to Scales himself the affair have already begin to appear less thoroughly jocose than he have anticipate . for he observe that Christian 's non - appearance before dinner have cause Mr. Debarry some consternation ; and he have gather that the courier have 133   be send on a commission to the rectory . " my uncle must have detain he for some reason or other , " he hear Mr. Philip say ; " but it be odd . if he be less trusty about commission , or have ever seem to drink too much , I should be uneasy . " altogether the affair be not take the turn Mr. Scales have intend . at last , when dinner have be remove , and the butler 's chief duty be at an end , it be understand that Christian have enter without his coat tail , look serious and even agitated ; that he have ask leave at once to speak to Mr. Debarry , and that he be even then in parley with the gentleman in the dining - room . Scales be in alarm ; it must have be some property of Mr. Debarry 's that have weight the pocket . he take a lantern , get a groom to accompany he with another lantern , and with the utmost practical speed reach the fatal spot in the park . he search under the elm he be certain that the pocket have fall there and he find the pocket ; but he find it empty , and , in spite of further search , do not find the content , though he have first console himself with think that they have fall out , and would be lie not far off . he return with the lantern and the coat tail and a most uncomfortable consciousness in that great seat of a butler 's emotion , the stomach . he have no soon re - enter than he be meet by Mrs. Cherry , pale and anxious , who draw he aside to say that if he do not   tell everything she would ; that the constable be to be send for ; that there have be no end of bank - note and letter and thing in Mr. Debarry 's pocket - book , which Christian be carry in that very pocket Scales have cut off ; that the rector be send for , the constable be come , and they should all be hang . Mr. Scales 's own intellect be anything but clear as to the possible issue . Crest - fall , and with the coat - tail in his hand as an attestation that he be innocent of anything more than a joke , he go and make his confession . his story relieve Christian a little , but do not relieve Mr. Debarry , who be more annoyed at the loss of the letter , and the chance of their get into hand that might make use of they , than at the loss of the bank - note . nothing could be do for the present , but that the rector , who be a magistrate , should instruct the constable , and that the spot in the park indicate by scale should again be carefully search . this be do , but in vain ; and many of the family at the Manor have disturb sleep that night .

 CHAPTER xiii . 

 " give sorrow leave awhile , to tutor I 
 to this submission . " Richard II . 
 meanwhile Felix Holt have be make his way back from Sproxton to Treby in some irritation and bitterness of spirit . for a little while he walk slowly along the direct road , hop that Mr. Johnson would overtake he , in which case he would have the pleasure of quarrel with he , and tell he what he think of his intention in come to can not at the Sugar Loaf . but he presently check himself in this folly and turn off again toward the canal , that he might avoid the temptation of get into a passion to no purpose . 

 " where be the good , " he think , " of pull at such a tangled skein as this electioneer trickery ? as long as three - fourth of the man in this country see nothing in an election but self - interest , and nothing in self - interest but some form of greed , one might as well try to purify the proceeding of the fish , and say to a hungry cod - fish ' my good friend , abstain ; do not   goggle your eye so , or show such a stupid gluttonous mouth , or think the little fish be worth nothing except in relation to your own inside . ' he would be open to no argument short of crimp he . I should get into a rage with this fellow , and perhaps end by thrash he . there be some reason in I as long as I keep my temper , but my rash humor be drunkenness without wine . I should not   wonder if he upset all my plan with these collier . of course he be go to treat they for the sake of get up a posse at the nomination and speechifying . they will drink double , and never come near I on a Saturday evening .
