 CHAPTER XXXIV . 

 the field be hoary with December 's frost , 
 I too be hoary with the chill of age . 
 but through the field and through the untrodden wood 
 be rest and stillness only in my heart 
 the pall of winter shroud a throb life . 
 a week after that Treby riot , Harold Transome be at Transome Court . he have return from a hasty visit to town to keep his Christmas at this delightful country home , not in the good Christmas spirit . he have lose the election ; but if that have be his only annoyance , he have good humor and good sense enough to have bear it as well as most man , and to have pay the eight or nine thousand , which have be the price of ascertain that he be not to sit in the next Parliament , without useless grumbling . but the disappointment of life can never , any more than its pleasure , be estimate singly ; and the healthy and most agreeable of man be expose to that coincidence of various vexation , each heighten the effect of the other , which may produce in he something correspond to the spontaneous and externally unaccountable moodiness of the morbid and disagreeable . 

 Harold might not have grieve much at a small riot in Treby , even if it have cause some expense to fall on the county ; but the turn which the riot have actually take be a bitter morsel for rumination , on more ground than one . however the disturbance have arise and be aggravate and probably no one know the whole truth on these point the conspicuous , grave incident have all tend to throw the blame on the Radical party , that be to say , on Transome and on Transome 's agent ; and so far the candidateship and its result have do Harold dishonor in the county : precisely the opposite effect to that 287   which be a dear object of his ambition . More than this , Harold 's conscience be active enough to be very unpleasantly affect by what have befall Felix Holt . his memory , always good , be particularly vivid in its retention of Felix Holt 's complaint to he about the treating of the Sproxton man , and of the subsequent irritate scene in Jermyn 's office , when the personage with the inauspicious name of Johnson have expound to he the impossibility of revise an electioneer scheme once begin , and of turn your vehicle back when it have already begin to roll downhill . Remembering Felix Holt 's word of indignant warning about hire man with drink in they to make a noise , Harold could not resist the urgent impression that the offence for which Felix be commit be fatality , not bring about by any willing co - operation of his with the noisy rioter , but arise probably from some rather ill - judge effort to counteract their violence . and this urgent impression , which insist on grow into a conviction , become in one of its phase an uneasy sense that he hold evidence which would at once tend to exonerate Felix and to place himself and his agent in anything but a desirable light . it be likely that some one else could give equivalent evidence in favor of Felix the little talkative Dissenting preacher , for example : but , anyhow , the affair with the Sproxton man would be rip open and make the bad of by the opposite party . the man who have fail in the use of some indirectness , be help very little by the fact that his rival be man to whom that indirectness be a something human , very far from be alien . there remain this grand distinction , that he have fail , and that the jet of light be throw entirely on his misdoing . 

 in this matter Harold feel himself a victim . could he hinder the trick of his agent ? in this particular case he have try to hinder they , and have try in vain . he have not love the two agent in question , to begin with ; and now at this later stage of event he be more innocent than ever of bear they anything but the most sincere ill - will . he be more utterly exasperate with they than he would probably have be if his one great passion have be for public virtue . Jermyn , with his John Johnson , have add this ugly , dirty business of the Treby election to all the long - accumulate list of offence , which Harold be resolve to visit on he to the utmost . he have see some handbill carry the insinuation that there be a discreditable indebtedness to Jermyn on 288   the part of the Transomes . if any such notion exist apart from electioneer slander , there be all the more reason for let the world see Jermyn severely punish for abuse his power over the family affair , and tamper with the family property . and the world certainly should see this with as little delay as possible . the cool , confident , assume fellow should be bleed to the last drop in compensation , and all connection with he be finally get rid of . now that the election be do with , Harold mean to devote himself to private affair , till everything lie in complete order under his supervision . 

 this morning he be seat as usual in his private room , which have now be handsomely fit up for he . it be but the third morning after the first Christmas he have spend in his english home for fifteen year , and the home look like an eminently desirable one . the white frost be now lie on the broad lawn , on the many - form leaf of the evergreen and on the giant tree at a distance . log of dry oak blaze on the hearth ; the carpet be like warm moss under his foot ; he have breakfast just accord to his taste , and he have the interesting occupation of a large proprietor to fill the morning . all through the house now step be noiseless on carpet or on fine matting ; there be warmth in hall and corridor ; there be servant enough to do everything , and to do it at the right time . Skilful Dominic be always at hand to meet his master 's demand , and his bland presence diffuse itself like a smile over the household , infect the gloomy english mind with the belief that life be easy , and make his real predominance seem as soft and light as a down quilt . old Mr. Transome have gather new courage and strength since little Harry and Dominic have come , and since Harold have insist on his take drive . Mrs. Transome herself be see on a fresh background with a gown of rich new stuff . and if , in spite of this , she do not seem happy , Harold either do not observe it , or kindly ignore it as the necessary frailty of elderly woman whose life have have too much of dullness and privation . our mind get trick and attitude as our body do , think Harold , and age stiffen they into unalterableness . " poor mother ! I confess I should not like to be an elderly woman myself . one require a good deal of the purr cat for that , or else of the love gran - dame . I wish she would take more to little Harry . I suppose she have her suspicion about the lad 's mother , and be as rigid in those matter as in her Toryism . however , I do what 289   I can ; it would be difficult to say what there be want to she in the way of indulgence and luxury to make up for the old niggardly life . " 

 and certainly Transome Court be now such a home as many woman would covet . yet even Harold 's own satisfaction in the midst of its elegant comfort need at present to be sustain by the expectation of gratified resentment . he be obviously less bright and enjoy than usual , and his mother , who watch he closely without dare to ask question , have gather hint and draw inference enough to make she feel sure that there be some storm gathering between he and Jermyn . she do not dare to ask question , and yet she have not resist the temptation to say something bitter about Harold 's failure to get return as a Radical , helping , with feminine self - defeat , to exclude herself more completely from any consultation by he . in this way poor woman , whose power lie solely in their influence , make themselves like music out of tune , and only move man to run away . 

 this morning Harold have order his letter to be bring to he at the breakfast - table , which be not his usual practice . his mother could see that there be London business letter about which he be eager , and she have find out that the letter bring by a clerk the day before be to make an appointment with Harold for Jermyn to come to Transome Court at eleven this morning . she observe Harold swallow his coffee and push away his plate with an early abstraction from the business of breakfast which be not at all after his usual manner . she herself eat nothing : her sip of tea seem to excite she ; her cheek flush , and her hand be cold . she be still young and ardent in her terror ; the passion of the past be live in her dread . 

 when Harold leave the table she go into the long drawing - room , where she might relieve her restlessness by walk up and down , and catch the sound of Jermyn 's entrance into Harold 's room , which be close by . here she move to and fro amongst the rose - colored satin of chair and curtain the great story of this world reduce for she to the little tale of her own existence dull obscurity everywhere , except where the keen light fall on the narrow track of her own lot , wide only for a woman 's anguish . at last she hear the expect ring and footstep , and the opening and closing door . unable to walk about any long , she sink into a large cushion chair , helpless and prayerless . she be not 290   thinking of God 's anger or mercy , but of her son 's . she be think of what might be bring , not by death , but by life .

 M. Check to your queen ! 
 N. Nay , your own king be bare ,

 CHAPTER XXXIX . 

 no man believe that many - texture knowledge and skill as a just idea of the solar system , or the power of paint flesh , or of read write harmony can come late and of a sudden ; yet many will not stick at believe that happiness can come at any day and hour solely by a new disposition of event ; though there be naught less capable of a magical production than a mortal 's happiness , which be mainly a complex of habitual relation and disposition not to be work by news from foreign part , or any whirling of fortune 's wheel for one on whose brow Time have write legibly .

 CHAPTER XL . 

 " she be beautiful ; and therefore to be woo : 
 she be a woman ; therefore to be win . " 
  Henry IV . 
 if Denner have have a suspicion that Esther 's presence at Transome Court be not agreeable to her mistress , it be impossible to entertain such a suspicion with regard to the other member of the family . between she and little Harry there be an extraordinary fascination . this creature , with the soft , broad , brown cheek , low forehead , great black eye , tiny , well - define nose , fierce , bite trick toward every person and thing he dislike , and insistence on entirely occupy those he like , be a human speciman such as Esther have never see before , and she seem to be equally original in Harry 's experience . at first sight her light complexion and her blue gown , probably also her sunny smile and her hand stretch out toward he , seem to make a show for he as of a new sort of bird : he throw himself backward against his " Gappa , " as he call old Mr. Transome , and star at this new comer with the gravity of a wild animal . but she have no soon sit down on the sofa in the 331   library than he climb up to she , and begin to treat she as an attractive object in natural history , snatch up her curl with his brown fist , and , discover that there be a little ear under they , pinch it and blow into it , pull at her coronet of plait , and seem to discover with satisfaction that it do not grow at the summit of her head , but could be drag down and altogether undo . then find that she laugh , toss he back , kiss , and pretend to bite he in fact , be an animal that understand fun he rush off and make Dominic bring a small menagerie of white mouse , squirrel , and bird , with Moro , the black spaniel , to make her acquaintance .  " he can talk well enough if he like , " say Gappa , evidently think that Harry , like the monkey , have deep reason for his reticence . 

 " you mind he , " he add , nod at Esther , and shake with low - toned laughter . " you will hear : he know the right name of thing well enough , but he like to make his own . he will give you one all to yourself before long . " 

 and when Harry seem to have make up his mind distinctly that Esther 's name be " Boo , " Mr. Transome nod at she with triumphant satisfaction , and then tell she in a low whisper , look round cautiously beforehand , that Harry would never call Mrs. Transome " Gamma , " but always " Bite . " 332 

 " it be wonderful ! " say he , laugh slyly . 

 the old man seem so happy now in the new world create for he by Dominic and Harry , that he would perhaps have make a holocaust of his fly and beetle if it have be necessary in order to keep this living , lively kindness about he . he no long confine himself to the library , but shuffle along from room to room , stay and look on at what be go forward whenever he do not find Mrs. Transome alone . 

 to Esther the sight of this feeble - minded , timid , paralytic man , who have long abdicate all mastery over the thing that be his , be something piteous . certainly this have never be part of the furniture she have imagine for the delightful aristocratic dwelling in her Utopia ; and the sad irony of such a lot impress she the more because in her father she be accustom to age accompany with mental acuman and activity . her thought go back in conjecture over the past life of Mr. and Mrs. Transome , a couple so strangely different from each other . she find it impossible to arrange their existence in the seclusion of this fine park and in this lofty large - roomed house , where it seem quite ridiculous to be anything so small as a human being , without find it rather dull . Mr. Transome have always have his beetle , but Mrs. Transome ? it be not easy to conceive that the husband and wife have ever be very fond of each other .

 after that morning on which Esther find herself redden and confuse by the sense of have make a distant allusion to Felix Holt , she feel it impossible that she should even , as she have sometimes intend , speak of he explicitly to Harold , in order to discuss probability as to the issue of his trial . she be certain she could not do it without betray emotion , and there be very complex reason in Esther 's mind why she could not bear that Harold should detect her sensibility on this subject . it be not only all the fibre of maidenly pride and reserve , of a bashfulness undefinably peculiar toward this man , who , while much old than herself , and bear the stamp of an experience quite hidden from her imagination , be take strongly the aspect of a lover it be not only this exquisite kind of shame which be at work within she : there be another sort of susceptibility in Esther , which her present circumstance tend to encourage , though she have come to regard it as not at all lofty , but rather as something which condemn she to littleness in comparison with a mind she have learn to venerate . she know quite well that , to Harold Transome , Felix Holt be one of the common people who could come into question in no other than a public light . she have a native capability for discern that the sense of rank and degree have its repulsion correspond to the repulsion dependent on difference of race and color ; and she remember her own impression too well not to foresee that it would come on Harold Transome as a shock , if he suspect there have be any love - passage between she and this young man , who to he be of course no more than any other intelligent member of the work class . I will tell the Court in as few word as I can , how I get entangle in the mob , how I come to attack the constable , and how I be lead to take a course which seem rather mad to myself , now I look back upon it .  Felix then give a concise narrative of his motif and conduct on the day of the riot , from the moment when he be startle into quit his work by the early uproar of the morning . he omit , of course , his visit to Malthouse Yard , and merely say that he go out to walk again after return to quiet his mother 's mind . he get warm by the story of his experience , which move he more strongly than ever , now he recall it in vibrate word before a large audience of his fellow - man . the sublime delight of truthful speech to one who have the great gift of utter it , will make itself feel even through the pang of sorrow . 

  389 

 " that be all I have to say for myself , my Lord . I plead ' not guilty ' to the charge of manslaughter , because I know that word may carry a meaning which would not fairly apply to my act . when I throw Tucker down , I do not see the possibility that he would die from a sort of attack which ordinarily occur in fighting without any fatal effect . as to my assault a constable , it be a quick choice between two evil : I should else have be disable . and he attack I under a mistake about my intention . I be not prepared to say I never would assault a constable where I have more chance of deliberation . I certainly should assault he if I see he do anything that make my blood boil : I reverence the law , but not where it be a pretext for wrong , which it should be the very object of law to hinder . I consider that I should be make an unworthy defence , if I let the Court infer from what I say myself , or from what be say by my witness , that because I be a man who hat drunken , motiveless disorder , or any wanton harm , therefore I be a man who would never fight against authority : I hold it blasphemy to say that a man ought not to fight against authority : there be no great religion and no great freedom that have not do it , in the beginning . it would be impertinent for I to speak of this now , if I do not need to say in my own defence , that I should hold myself the bad sort of traitor if I put my hand to either fighting or disorder which must mean to injure somebody if I be not urge to it by what I hold to be sacred feeling , make a sacred duty either to my own manhood or to my fellow - man . and certainly , " Felix end , with a strong ring of scorn in his voice , " I never hold it a sacred duty to try and get a radical candidate return for North Loamshire , by willingly head a drunken howl mob , whose public action must consist in break window , destroy hard - got produce , and endanger the life of man and woman . I have no more to say , my Lord . " 

 " I foresaw he would make a blunder , " say Harold , in a low voice to Esther . then , see she shrink a little , he fear she might suspect he of be merely sting by the allusion to himself . " I do not   mean what he say about the radical candidate , " he add , hastily , in correction . " I do not   mean the last sentence . I mean that whole peroration of his , which he ought to have leave unsaid . it have do he harm with the jury they will not   understand it , or rather will misunderstand it . 390   and I will answer for it , it have sour the judge . it remain to be see what we witness can say for he , to nullify the effect of what he have say for himself . I hope the attorney have do his good in collect the evidence : I understand the expense of the witness be undertake by some liberal at Glasgow and in Lancashire , friend of Holt 's . but I suppose your father have tell you . " 

 the first witness call to the defence be Mr. Lyon . the gist of his statement be , that from the beginning of September last till the day of the election he be in very frequent intercourse with the prisoner ; that he have become intimately acquaint with his character and view of life , and his conduct with respect to the election , and that these be totally inconsistent with any other supposition than his be involve in the riot , and his fatal encounter with the constable , be due to the calamitous failure of a bold but good purpose . he state far that he have be present when an interview have occur in his own house between the prisoner and Mr. Harold Transome , who be then canvass for the representation of North Loamshire . that the object of the prisoner in seek this interview have be to inform Mr. Transome of treat give in his name to the workman in the pit and on the canal at Sproxton , and to remonstrate against its continuance ; the prisoner fear that disturbance and mischief might result from what he believe to be the end toward which this treating be direct namely , the presence of these man on the occasion of the nomination and polling . several time after this interview , Mr. Lyon say , he have hear Felix Holt recur to the subject therein discuss with expression of grief and anxiety . he himself be in the habit of visit Sproxton in his ministerial capacity : he know fully what the prisoner have do there in order to found a night school , and be certain that the prisoner 's interest in the workingmen of that district turn entirely on the possibility of convert they somewhat to habit of soberness and to a due care for the instruction of their child . finally , he state that the prisoner , in compliance with his request , have be present at Duffield on the day of the nomination , and have on his return express himself with strong indignation concern the employment of the Sproxton man on that occasion , and what he call the wickedness of hire blind violence . 

 the quaint appearance and manner of the little dissent minister could not fail to stimulate the peculiar wit of the bar . he be subject to 391   a troublesome cross - examination , which he bear with wide - eyed short - sighted quietude and absorption in the duty of truthful response . on be ask rather sneeringly , if the prisoner be not one of his flock ? he answer , in that deep tone which make one of the most effective transition of his vary voice 

 " Nay would to God he be ! I should then feel that the great virtue and the pure life I have behold in he be a witness to the efficacy of the faith I believe in and the discipline of the Church whereunto I belong . " 

 perhaps it require a large power of comparison than be possess by any of that audience to appreciate the moral elevation of an Independent minister who could utter those word . nevertheless there be a murmur which be clearly one of sympathy .

Harold be keenly alive to this , and to everything else that might prove disagreeable to he in his have to appear in the witness - box . but he be not likely to lose his self - possession , or to fail in adjust himself gracefully , under condition which most man would find it difficult to carry without awkwardness . he have generosity and candor enough to bear Felix Holt 's proud rejection of his advance without any petty resentment ; he have all the susceptibility of a gentleman ; and these moral quality give the right direction to his acuman , in judging of the behavior that would well secure his dignity . everything require self - command be easy to he because of Esther 's presence ; for her admiration be just then the object which this well - tan man of the world have it most at heart to secure .

 Harold speak with as noticeable directness and emphasis , as if what he say could have no reaction on himself . he have of course not enter unnecessarily into what occur in Jermyn 's office . but now he be subject to a cross - examination on this subject , which give rise to some subdue shrug , smile , and wink , among county gentleman .

 the next witness be Michael Brincey , otherwise Mike Brindle , who give evidence of the saying and doing of the prisoner among the Sproxton man .  " I be Esther Lyon , the daughter of Mr. Lyon , the Independent minister at Treby , who have be one of the witness for the prisoner .  a little after five o'clock that day , Harold arrive at Transome Court . as he be wind along the broad road of the park , some parting gleam of the March sun pierce the tree here and there , and throw on the grass a long shadow of himself and the groom riding , and illuminate a window or two of the home he be approach . but the bitterness in his mind make these sunny gleam almost as odious as an artificial smile . he wish he have never come back to this pale English sunshine .

 Felix stand a moment or two , dwell on she with a face where the gravity gather tenderness . 

 " and these curl ? " he say , with a sort of relent , seat himself again , and put his hand on they . 

 " they cost nothing they be natural . " 

 " you be such a delicate creature . " 

 " I be very healthy . poor woman , I think , be healthy than the rich . besides , " Esther go on , with a mischievous meaning , " I think of have some wealth . " 

 " how ? " say Felix , with an anxious start . " what do you mean ? " 

 " I think even of two pound a week : one need not   live up to the splendor of all that , you know ; we might live as simply as you like : there would be money to spare , and you could do wonder , and be oblige to work too , only not if sickness come . and then I think of a little income for 418   your mother , enough for she to live as she have be use to live ; and a little income for my father , to save he from be dependent when he be no long able to preach . " 

 Esther say all this in a playful tone , but she end , with a grave look of appeal submission 

 " I mean if you approve . I wish to do what you think it will be right to do . " 

 Felix put his hand on her shoulder again and reflect a little while , look on the hearth : then he say , lift up his eye , with a smile at her 

 " why , I shall be able to set up a great library , and lend the book to be dog's - eare and mark with bread - crumb . " 

 Esther say , laugh , " you think you be to be everything . you do not   know how clever I be . I mean to go on teach a great many thing . " 

 " teach I ? " 

 " oh , yes , " she say , with a little toss ; " I shall improve your french accent . " 

 " you will not   want I to wear a stock , " say Felix , with a defiant shake of the head . 

 " no ; and you will not attribute stupid thought to I before I have   utter they . " 

 they laugh merrily , each hold the other 's arm , like girl and boy . there be the ineffable sense of youth in common . 

 then Felix lean forward , that their lip might meet again , and after that his eye rove tenderly over her face and curl . 

 " I be a rough , severe fellow , Esther . shall you never repent ? never be inwardly reproach I that I be not a man who could have share your wealth ? be you quite sure ? " 

 " quite sure ! " say Esther , shake her head ; " for then I should have honor you less . I be weak my husband must be great and noble than I be . " 

 " oh , I tell you what , though ! " say Felix , start up , thrust his hand into his pocket , and crease his brow playfully , " if you take I in that way I shall be force to be a much well fellow than I ever think of be . " 

 " I call that retribution , " say Esther , with a laugh as sweet as the morning thrush . 

  419 

 EPILOGUE . 

 our fine hope be fine memory ; 
 and those who love in age think youth be happy , 
 because it have a life to fill with love . 
 the very next May , Felix and Esther be marry . every one in those day be marry at the parish church ; but Mr. Lyon be not satisfied without an additional private solemnity , " wherein there be no bondage to questionable form , so that he might have a more enlarged utterance of joy and supplication . " 

 it be a very simple wedding ; but no wedding , even the gay , ever raise so much interest and debate in Treby Magna . even very great people , like Sir Maximus and his family , go to the church to look at this bride , who have renounce wealth , and choose to be the wife of a man who say he would always be poor . 

 some few shake their head ; could not quite believe it ; and think there be " more behind . " but the majority of honest Trebians be affect somewhat in the same way as happy - look Mr. Wace be , who observe to his wife , as they walk from under the churchyard chestnut , " it be wonderful how thing go through you you do not   know how . I feel somehow as if I believe more in everything that be good . " 

 Mrs. Holt , that day , say she feel herself to be receive ' some reward , " imply that justice certainly have much more in reserve . little Job Tudge have an entirely new suit , of which he finger every separate brass button in a way that threaten an arithmetical mania ; and Mrs. Holt have out her good tea - tray and put down her carpet again , with the satisfaction of think that there would no more be boy come in all weather with dirty shoe . 

 for Felix and Esther do not take up their abode in Treby Magna ; and after a while Mr. Lyon leave the town too , and join they where they dwelt . on his resignation the church in Malthouse Yard choose a successor to he whose doctrine be rather high . 

 there be other departure from Treby . Mr. Jermyn 's establishment be break up , and he be understand to have go to reside at a great distance : some say " abroad , " that large home of ruined reputation . Mr. Johnson continue blonde and sufficiently prosperous till he get gray and rather more prosperous . some person who do not think highly 420   of he , hold that his prosperity be a fact to be keep in the background , as be dangerous to the moral of the young ; judge that it be not altogether creditable to the Divine Providence that anything but virtue should be reward by a front and back drawing - room in Bedford row . 

 as for Mr. Christian , he have no more profitable secret at his disposal . but he get his thousand pound from Harold Transome . 

 the Transome family be absent some time from Transome Court . the place be keep up and show to visitor , but not by Denner , who be away with her mistress . after a while the family come back , and Mrs. Transome die there . Sir Maximus be at her funeral , and throughout that neighborhood there be silence about the past . 

 Uncle Lingon continue to watch over the shooting on the Manor and the cover until that event occur which he have predict as a part of Church reform sure to come . Little Treby have a new rector , but other be sorry besides the old pointer . 

 as to all that wide parish of Treby Magna , it have since prosper as the rest of England have prosper . Doubtless there be more enlightenment now . whether the farmer be all public - spirited , the shopkeeper nobly independent , the Sproxton man entirely sober and judicious , the Dissenters quite without narrowness or asperity in religion and politic , and the publican all fit , like Gaius , to be the friend of an apostle these thing I have not hear ; not have correspondence in those part . whether any presumption may be draw from the fact that North Loamshire do not yet return a radical candidate , I leave to the all - wise I mean the newspaper . 

 as to the town in which Felix Holt now reside , I will keep that a secret , lest he should be trouble by any visitor have the insufferable motive of curiosity . 

 I will only say that Esther have never repent . Felix , however , grumble a little that she have make his life too easy , and that , if it be not for much walking , he should be a sleek dog . 

 there be a young Felix , who have a great deal more science than his father , but not much more money . 


 the END .
